Compare French sirop, Italian siroppo, Spanish jarabe, jarope, Portuguese xar...

straffen.e: From Middle Dutch straffen, probably from Middle High German strāfen, which may go back to Proto-West Germanic *strēpōn, of uncertain ultimate origin, but possibly related to *streupan (“to squeeze, press, graze”). The short vowel and geminate in Dutch in contrast to the Middle High German long v...

samen.e: From Middle Dutch tsamen, a contraction of earlier tesamen, from te samen. Although not clear from the spelling, the ⟨s⟩ in the Middle Dutch word was pronounced as voiced /z/. That is, (te)samen was pronounced /(tə)zaːmən/, like the modern descendant tezamen and the related words gezamenlijk and ...

symposium.e: From Ancient Greek συμπόσιον (sumpósion, “drinking party”) from συμπίνω (sumpínō, “drink together”) συν- (sun-, “together-”) + πίνω (pínō, “drink”).

sneeuw.e:
From Middle Dutch snêe, from Old Dutch snēo, from Proto-West Germanic *snaiw, from Proto-Germanic *snaiwaz.

Compare construct, instruct, destroy, etc.

scheiden.e: From Middle Dutch scheiden, from Old Dutch skeithan, from Proto-West Germanic *skaiþan, from Proto-Germanic *skaiþaną.

stom.e: From Middle Dutch stom, from Old Dutch stum, from Proto-West Germanic *stumm, from Proto-Germanic *stummaz. Cognate with German stumm, Old Saxon stum.

From Middle Dutch stedes, steeds, an adverbial genitive of stede (“steadfast, durable”), with umlaut from the more common stade, from Old Dutch *stādi, from Proto-Germanic *stēdi-.

schoonbroer.e: From schoon + broer, calque of Middle French beau-frere.

snaphaan.e: Borrowed from German Schnapphahn (“robber on horseback”). Equivalent to snappen (“to get, to apprehend”) + haan (“rooster, cock”).

snoep.e: Back-formation from snoepen. Attested from the 17th century.

s.def: a kind of diacritic used on Ancient Greek vowels to indicate aspiration or lack thereof. See spiritus asper and spiritus lenis

stadion.e: Learned borrowing from Ancient Greek στάδιον (stádion), from στάδιος (stádios), from Proto-Indo-European *steh₂-.

s.def: synonym of vetkruid (“stonecrop, succulent plant of genus Sedum”)
pike (any fish of the genus Esox)

snoek.e: From Middle Dutch snoec, from Old Dutch *snuoc, from Proto-Germanic *snōkᵏa-, a thematic o-grade of *snakaną (“to crawl around”), related to *sneganą. Related to English snake.

santa cruz.e: Borrowed from Spanish Santa Cruz (“Holy Cross”).

s.def: partitive of slapper, the comparative degree of slap

sprong.e: From Middle Dutch sprong, from Old Dutch sprunc, from Proto-West Germanic *sprung; cognate with German Sprung. An ablauted (zero-grade) verbal noun of springen (“to jump, leap”).

examples: Sores komen vanzelf, simches moet je vieren.

sores.e: Borrowed from Yiddish צרות (tsores), plural of צרה (tsore, “trouble, problem”), from Hebrew צָרָה (tsará, “trouble, tragedy, calamity”).

s.def: somersault, flip (jump where one makes a 360° rotation)

salto.e: Borrowed from Italian salto, from Latin saltus. First attested in the 18th century.

examples: Het contest stond vooral open voor jongemannen met Saamaka roots, maar het door hen gepresenteerde geldt grotendeels ook voor andere stammen.

stam.e: From Middle Dutch stam, from Old Dutch *stam, from Proto-West Germanic *stamni, from Proto-Germanic *stamniz.

s.def: a male or female given name from Japanese

souteneur.e: From French souteneur (literally “one who supports”), from the verb soutenir.

s.def: a top, a peak, a pinnacle or an apex
a striker (both the position and the player), often referring to the centre forward
a frontline battalion, a front subdivision of a vanguard

sla.e: From earlier slae, slaê, with the first syllable reduced from earlier salae, salaê, it in turn being a reduced form of salade with regular loss of intervocalic -d- (as happened in words such as slee, broer, and teer). The semantic distinction between both forms is secondary.

examples: Coordinate term: spa blauw

spa rood.e: From the Belgian mineral water brand Spa + rood (“red”).

examples: Coordinate term: spa rood

spa blauw.e: From the Belgian mineral water brand Spa + blauw (“blue”).

s.def: one of the lights on a traffic light or on another signalling device, in particular a red one

stoplicht.e: Compound of stop + licht. Cf. English stop light.

examples: Vandaag maakt Stefan zijn huis schoon.

schoonmaken.e: From Middle Dutch schoonmaken, equivalent to schoon (“clean”) + maken (“to make”).

s.def: skeleton (support structure of an organism; ensemble of animal bones)
frame, skeleton (supporting frame of an inanimate or non-animal object or entity)

skelet.e: Borrowed from German Skelett, from Ancient Greek σκελετός (skeletós, “dried up, withered, dried body, parched, mummy”), from σκελλώ (skellṓ, “to dry, dry up, to make dry, to parch”), from Proto-Indo-European *(s)kelh₁- (“to parch, wither”).

schoen.e: From Middle Dutch schoe, from Old Dutch skuo, from Proto-West Germanic *skōh, from Proto-Germanic *skōhaz. The original Middle Dutch plural was schoen, which was reanalysed as a singular form.

examples: Voor begrafenis, bruiloft of dopen droegen de mannen een donkerblauw kostuum met een witte streep, wit overhemd en stropdas.

schilderij.e: From the stem of schilderen (“to paint”) + -erij (“-ery”). Or alternatively, from schilder (“painter”) + -ij (“-y”). See also the usage notes at -erij.

schilderen.e: From Middle Dutch schilderen. Equivalent to schilder + -en. In medieval times, shields were often painted in the coat of arms of the wearer, the person who did this was named schilder. This person often took on the task of painting other things.

s.def: ticket for local public transport (i.e. for buses, trams, and metro)

strippenkaart.e: From strip + -en- + kaart. Named because it consists of strips that are stamped according to the fare to be paid.

segment.e: Borrowed from French segment, from Latin segmentum.

silbe.e: Borrowed from German Silbe, from Old High German sillaba, from Latin syllaba, from Ancient Greek συλλαβή (sullabḗ).

s.def: aid, help; the act of helping

subsidie.e: From Middle Dutch subsidie, from Latin subsidium.

examples: De klok op het stationsgebouw wijst de tijd aan.

s.def: an amount of money to keep close at hand for emergencies (usually in the diminutive)
a supercomputer, type of computer that has great processing power

sporangium.e: From Late Latin sporangium, from Ancient Greek σπορά (sporá, “seed”) + ἀγγεῖον (angeîon, “vessel”).

s.def: to pass a test or examination

slagen.e: Formed from the past participle of slaan.

s.def: drat, darn; Used as an expression of frustration, if something doesn't work out as expected. It is a rather innocent, child-friendly curse.
examples: Stik, alweer ernaast!

serendipiteit.e: From Serendip + -iteit. Voor het eerst gebruikt door Horace Walpole, 1754.

sollicitatie.e: Borrowed from French sollicitation, from Latin sollicitātiō. Equivalent to solliciteren + -atie.

s.def: a doctor's office, a surgery, a consulting room, a consultation room

stoer.e: Originally the same word as stuurs, of which it is an eastern dialectal variant. Immediately cognate with German stur (itself from Low German). Not immediately cognate with English stour, Swedish stor, but possibly related.

examples: stuur het leger!

sturen.e: From Middle Dutch sturen, from Old Dutch stiuren, from Proto-West Germanic *stiurijan, from Proto-Germanic *stiurijaną.

s.def: a steering device, notably a steering wheel in a vehicle or handlebars on a bicycle
(used absolutely, with the definite article: het stuur) Control

stuur.e: From Middle Dutch stier, stuur, from the root of Etymology 2 below. For the obsolete sense “tax”, compare German Steuer; the original meaning was "support."

s.def: sensualist (person who believes in enjoying sensuality)
sensualist (one who holds to the doctrine of sensualism)

supermarkt.e: From super- + markt, calque of English supermarket.

s.def: oral presentation, lecture as a school project

stiekem.e: Borrowed from Yiddish שתיקה (shtike), from Hebrew שתיקה (shtika, “silence”), see שָׁתַק (“to be silent”). Compare regional German stiekum.

slikken.e: From Middle Dutch slicken, from Old Dutch *slikkon, from Proto-Germanic *slikkōną.

examples: We hebben een serieus probleem.

serieus.e: From Middle French sérieux. Doublet of yusu.

slechts.e: From Middle Dutch slechtes (“simply”). Equivalent to slecht (“simple”, obsolete sense) + -s (adverbial suffix).

strategisch.e: Perhaps borrowed from German strategisch. Equivalent to strategie + -isch.

s.def: society (long-standing group of people sharing cultural aspects; people of one’s country or community taken as a whole)
skinhead (member of a punk subculture characterised by a shaved head)

straf.e: Early modern Dutch straffe, derived from straffen. Compare German Strafe.

skateboarder.e: Borrowed from English skateboarder. Equivalent to skateboarden + -er.

s.def: The act of sliding or gliding.
temple (slightly flatter region on either side of the human head)

slaap.e: From Middle Dutch slâep, from Old Dutch *slāp, from Proto-West Germanic *slāp.

examples: Stelling van Amsterdam

stelling.e: From Middle Dutch stellinge. Equivalent to stellen + -ing.

sfeer.e: From Middle Dutch spere, from Middle French spere, from Old French spere, from Medieval Latin spera, from sphaera, from Ancient Greek σφαῖρα (sphaîra).

s.def: to shrink, to lessen, to decrease

slinken.e: From Middle Dutch slinken, from Old Dutch *slinkan, from Proto-Germanic *slinkaną.

s.def: a singlet, short, sleeveless type of shirt, worn alone (as in sports) or as undershirt; originally only for men

spijkeren.e: From Middle Dutch spikeren. Equivalent to spijker + -en.

examples: Schoonheid is zo relatief; wat de een mooi vindt, vindt de ander superlelijk.
Waar ga je naartoe, schoonheid?

schoonheid.e: From Middle Dutch schôonheit, from Old Dutch [Term?]. Equivalent to schoon (“beautiful”) + -heid (“-ness, -hood”).

s.def: a tool used for shovelling or digging
iron, smoothing iron (device for pressing clothes)
a racing cyclist who is bad at sprinting

strijkijzer.e: From strijken + ijzer. The cycling sense derives from French fer à repasser.

strijken.e: From Middle Dutch striken, from Old Dutch *strīcan, from Proto-West Germanic *strīkan, from Proto-Germanic *strīkaną, from Proto-Indo-European *streyg-.

examples: Ik heb daar echt skitta aan.

skitta.e: From Sranan Tongo sket (“to defecate”); see also schijt.

s.def: speech (act, manner or faculty of speaking)
examples: Hij heeft zijn spraak verloren.
Zijn spraak was duidelijk en goed verstaanbaar.

spraak.e: From earlier sprake, from Middle Dutch sprâke, from Old Dutch sprāca, from Proto-West Germanic *sprākkijā, *sprāku; the word thus ultimately represents an ablauted derivation, with lengthened vowel (vṛddhi), from the same root as spreken.

s.def: tortoise, turtle; reptile of the order Testudines
examples: Because of its cheapness I wanted to ask him to give me only that feed without any turtle then, but he interrupted me: […]

2006, Nettie Dees, Anders dan je denkt, Callenbach (publ.), page 92.
Ze pakte een schildpadje op.

s.def: tortoise, cat, testudo (wheeled gallery offering protection to approaching besiegers; siege weapon)
examples: Het gewigtigste Werktuig tot bedekking van de Arbeiders, byzonder wanneer dezelve de gragten opvulden, den grond slechteden, of ook wallen moesten opwerpen, was de zoogenaamde Schildpad, of het Stormdak.
De gracht, die 7 el diep en 14 el breed was, werd door middel van drie Schildpadden gevuld.

1745, Algemeene histori, vol. XII, parts I and II, page 872.
Maar Vitellius volk brak de ſchildpad door grote ſtéénen daar op te werpen, velde het volk, dat 'er order was, en maakte een ſchriklijke verwoeſting onder hen, die dus blóót en voor de vluchten ſtéénen en pijlen ongedekt waren.
[…] maar toen de huizen krakend instortten, riep hij met luide stem zijn soldaten, zich rondom hem te verzamelen en hun schilden aaneen te sluiten tot een zoogenaamde schildpad of testudo.

s.def: ship's block (one or more pulleys in a shell)
examples:
1859, G. P. J. Mossel. Handleiding tot de kennis van het schip, wed. G. Hulst van Keulen (publ.), page 253.
Om ruimte te winnen, naait men dus tegen den voorkant van dien mast eene groote schildpad met twee schijven boven elkander voor de kruisschoten.
In een of meer schijfgaten in de schildpad zijn pokhouten of metalen schijven geplaatst, die om een verticalen ijzeren nagel draaien.

schildpad.e: From Middle Dutch schiltpadde, compare the slightly older Middle Low German schildepadde. Equivalent to schild (“shield”) + pad (“toad”).

schrijver.e: From Middle Dutch schriver. Equivalent to schrijven + -er.

scheikunde.e: From scheiden + -kunde. Purism introduced by Simon Stevin in the 16th century to replace the Dutch word chemie (“chemistry”) which was a borrowing from Latin chimia.

s.def: to practice sports in general, to play a sport, to exercise

sporten.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

sporter.e: Either from sporten + -er or borrowed from English sporter. The verb sporten is attested a few years later than sporter.

s.def: to support, keep up, back up, assist
to rest, be based or founded on

steunen.e: From Middle Dutch stōnen, from Old Dutch *stonōn, *stunōn, from Proto-West Germanic *stunōn (“to sound, crash, bang, strike, knock”), from Proto-Germanic *stunōną (“to make a loud sound, thunder, crash, rumble, groan”), from Proto-Indo-European *(s)tenh₂- (“to thunder”).

schepen.e: From Middle Dutch schēpen, from Old Dutch scepeno, possibly a derivative of Frankish *skappjan (“to shape, create”). Cognate with German Schöffe.

slachtoffer.e: Compound of slachten + offer. Compare German Schlachtopfer.

slacht.e: From Middle Dutch slacht, from Old Dutch slaht, from Proto-West Germanic *slahtu, from Proto-Germanic *slahtuz. Equivalent to slaan (“to hit, strike”) + -t (“verbal noun”).

slachten.e: From Middle Dutch slachten, from Old Dutch *slahton, from Proto-Germanic *slahtōną, related to *slahaną (“to hit, strike”). Cognate with German schlachten.

situatie.e: From Middle Dutch situacie, from Middle French situation, ultimately from Latin situātiō: equivalent to situeren + -atie.

statie.e: Variant of station, based on other words in which French -ion corresponds to Dutch -ie.

examples: schuin achter mij – diagonally behind me

schuin.e: Origin uncertain. Apparently related to German Low German schüün (“slanting, sloping”), Saterland Frisian schüün (“slanting, sloping”), West Frisian skean (“slanting, sloping”), Middle English askoin (“at a slant, askance”), Norwegian skeina (“to move obliquely”).

spellen.e: From Middle Dutch spellen, from Old Dutch *spellon, from Proto-Germanic *spellōną.

s.def: to suspend, delay (an event, proceeding) by inserting a pause
examples: De voorzitter schorst de zitting.

s.def: to suspend, temporarily depose / remove from office or from membership or enrolment in an organisation
examples: De verdachten worden ambtshalve geschorst.

schorsen.e: From Middle Dutch schorsen, from Old French escorcier, from Vulgar Latin *excurtiō.

s.def: skate, in-line skate (originally one with five wheels, but soon also used for four-wheel in-line skates)

examples: Ik ben gestart met roken, toen ik vijftien jaar oud was.

starten.e: From start + -en (modeled on the English verb start); compare with German starten.

examples: Er heerste een hevige strijd tussen de rivaliserende bendes.
De politieke partijen voerden een felle strijd om de gunst van de kiezers.
De soldaten waren moe van de lange dagen van strijd op het slagveld.
De luchtmacht voerde een succesvolle strijd tegen de vijandelijke vliegtuigen.

strijd.e:
From Middle Dutch strijt, from Old Dutch *strīt, from Proto-West Germanic *strīd, from Proto-Germanic *strīdaz.
Cognate to Old Saxon strīd, Old High German strīt, German Streit, Old Frisian strīd, Old Norse stríð.

s.def: to fight, to wage battle or war
to fight, to engage in conflict or competition

strijden.e: From Middle Dutch striden, from Old Dutch strīdan, from Proto-West Germanic *strīdan, from Proto-Germanic *strīdaną.

schaats.e: From Middle Dutch schāetse (“stilt”), from Old Northern French escache (“a stilt, trestle”), from Frankish *skakkjā (“stilt”, literally “thing that moves”), from the verb *skakan (“to shake”).

spugen.e: Reformed from spuwen, by analogy with its older strong past tense spoog.

s.def: split-second (fraction of a second; a very short time period; an instant, a moment.)
border (over de schreef: across the border)
a decoration at the end of lines which make up letters; a serif

schreef.e: From Middle Dutch screve, ultimately from the root of schrijven (“to write”).

s.def: imminent, arriving or occurring soon, soon

spoedig.e: From Middle Dutch spoedich. Equivalent to spoed + -ig.

seleen.e: A borrowing from German Selen or Swedish selen, or a shortening of selenium, as seleen is attested considerably later than selenium.

s.def: to be convenient for (e.g. of an appointment time)
examples: Schikt het als ik morgen even langskom?

s.def: to settle, resolve a legal dispute
examples: Trump schikt met omroep ABC.

schikken.e: From Middle Dutch schicken. Further etymology unclear, found only in continental West Germanic. Cognates are Middle Low German schicken, German schicken.

s.def: regiolect, regional language (used for both dialects and separate languages with a regional presence)
fuel oil, marine oil, furnace oil, heavy fuel

stabiliseren.e: Borrowed from French stabiliser. Equivalent to stabiel + -iseren.

s.def: to bring into a certain mood
examples: Ons offer zal de goden gunstig stemmen.
De nieuwsgierige stokstaartjes moeten even wennen aan de gekleurde eieren, maar lieten het zich toch goed smaken.
De stichting van de stad Rome vond in 753 voor Christus plaats.
Onze organisatie is een stichting, dus we zijn geheel zonder winstoogmerk.

stichting.e: From Middle Dutch stichtinge. By surface analysis, stichten + -ing. Compare German Stiftung.

stichten.e: From Middle Dutch stichten, from Old Dutch stiften, stihten, from Proto-Germanic *stiftijaną.

s.def: shrew, laurasiatherian of the family Soricidae

spitsmuis.e: From spits + muis, calque of German Spitzmaus. First attested around 1600.

schaakstuk.e: Compound of schaak or schaken + stuk.

scheen.e: From Middle Dutch schene, from Old Dutch *skina, from Proto-West Germanic *skinu, from Proto-Germanic *skinō. Cognate with German Schiene, English shin.

examples: het stoffelijk overschot.

solfège.e: Borrowed from French solfège, from Italian solfeggio.

s.def: to compose, to make up, to form

stel.e: From Middle Dutch stel. By surface analysis, deverbal from stellen.

scheepvaart.e: From Middle Dutch schipvaert, scheepvaert, from schip (“ship”) + vaert (“travelling”). Equivalent to modern schip + vaart.

stoten.e: From Middle Dutch stôten, from Old Dutch stōtan, from Proto-West Germanic *stautan, from Proto-Germanic *stautaną.

s.def: to interfere with, to cause interference

storen.e: From Middle Dutch stôren, from Old Dutch *stōren, from Proto-West Germanic *staurijan.

slepen.e: From Middle Dutch slêpen, from Old Dutch *slēpen, from Proto-West Germanic *slaipijan.

stikstof.e: Borrowing or calque of German Stickstoff, from stikken (“to suffocate”) + stof (“matter”), referring to the fact that an animal placed on pure nitrogen suffocates.

examples: Hij liep na dat ongeluk met een stok.

stok.e: From Middle Dutch stoc, from Old Dutch stok, from Frankish *stokk, from Proto-Germanic *stukkaz.

s.def: to determine the gender (of young animals)
a city and municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Vijfheerenlanden, Utrecht, Netherlands
a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

sluis.e: From Middle Dutch sluse, from Old Dutch slūsa, from Late Latin exclūsa, sclūsa (“sluice”), from exclūsus, form of exclūdō (“I shut out, I exclude”). Cognate to English sluice.

s.def: trunk (extended nasal organ of certain animals, like the elephant)
obsolete form of slurp (“slurpy gulp; slurpy noise”)

slurf.e: From slurven (a variant of slurpen (“to slurp”)) via Middle Dutch slorf.

s.def: common starling (Sturnus vulgaris, individually or specifically)
starling (passerine bird of the family Sturnidae)

spreeuw.e: From Middle Dutch spreeuwe. Related to Saterland Frisian Spräie. North Frisian Sprian, Dutch Low Saxon Spraoje, Low German Spree.

s.def: Sanaa (the capital and largest city of Yemen)

examples: Zijn vertrek smartte mij zeer.

smarten.e: From Middle Dutch smarten, smerten, from Old Dutch *smertan, from Proto-West Germanic *smertan, from Proto-Germanic *smertaną.

s.def: a season, major part of the year
a season, term or period used for a certain activity or when something occurs

seizoen.e: From Middle Dutch saisoene, sysoen, from Old French saison, from Latin satiōnem.

stengel.e: Borrowed from Middle High German stengel, from Old High German stengil, from Proto-West Germanic *stangil, from Proto-Germanic *stangilaz. By surface analysis, stang + -el, with umlaut.

s.def: to skate (especially with in-line skates or a skateboard)
the tree frog species Boana faber, whose call resounds like a smith's hammer coming down
a surname originating as an occupation, equivalent to Smith in English

smid.e: From Middle Dutch smit, from Old Dutch *smith, from Proto-West Germanic *smiþ, from Proto-Germanic *smiþaz.

s.def: spy, person who secretly gathers information
a dog breed for the partridge hunt
spray (liquid commercial product sold in a spray container)

examples: Maar nu heb ik die sani's in mijn onderbroek

s.def: to yield, make money, to earn money (for someone)
examples: Hoeveel schuift dat?

schuiven.e: From Middle Dutch schuven, from Old Dutch *scūvan, from Proto-West Germanic *skeuban, from Proto-Germanic *skeubaną, from Proto-Indo-European *skewbʰ-. Doublet of sjouwen.

s.def: debit and credit side of a balance sheet

examples: samengesteld blad

s.def: expression used to wish someone the strength to persevere whatever hardship they are experiencing (usually illness, or the death of a loved one)
examples: Gecondoleerd met het verlies van je opa. Heel veel sterkte!

sterkte.e: From Middle Dutch stercte, sterkede, from Old Dutch *sterkitha, from Proto-West Germanic *starkiþu. By surface analysis, sterk + -te.

s.def: to encourage, to give strength to
examples: Sterkte ermee!

sterken.e: From Middle Dutch sterken, from Old Dutch *sterken, from Proto-West Germanic *starkijan.

schelp.e: From Middle Dutch schelpe, from Old Dutch *skella, Proto-West Germanic *skallju, from Proto-Germanic *skaljō (“shell”).

snede.e: From Middle Dutch snede. See the verb snijden (“to cut”).

s.def: key: an object designed to fit between two other objects (such as a shaft and a wheel) in a mechanism and maintain their relative orientation.

schelden.e: From Middle Dutch schelden, from Old Dutch skeldan, from Proto-Germanic *skeldaną.

s.def: to cause regret to, to cause to be sorry
examples: Zijn daden speten hem.
Het spijt me.

spijten.e: From Middle Dutch spiten, equivalent to spijt + -en.

schuilen.e: From Middle Dutch schulen, from Old Dutch *skūlen, from Proto-West Germanic *skūlijan, from Proto-Germanic *skūlijaną.

snuiven.e: From Middle Dutch snuyven, from Old Dutch *snūvan, from Proto-West Germanic *sneufan, from Proto-Germanic *sneufaną (“to breathe, puff, blow, snort”), imitative of drawing air through the nose, related to Middle High German snūfen, German schnauben.

sproeien.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch sproeyen. Compare English spray.

s.def: to be startled, to get a scare
examples: Niet schrikken!

schrikken.e: From Middle Dutch schricken, from Old Dutch *skrikken, from Proto-Germanic *skrikjaną; the ultimate origin is uncertain, possibly related to *skeraną (“to shear”) or *skrīaną (“to scream”). Also compare Old Norse skaga (“to jut out”).

s.def: a prick (insect bite), a stab, a thrust
a bicorne or tricorn; a cornered hat

steek.e: From Middle Dutch stēke, from Old Dutch *stiki, from Proto-West Germanic *stiki.

s.def: to suffocate, to cause problems with breathing
to teem (to have an abundance of)
examples: Uche uche uche uche uche, / het stikt hier van de muggen.

stikken.e: From Middle Dutch sticken, from Old Dutch *sticcon, from Proto-West Germanic *stikkōn, from Proto-Germanic *stikkōną.

s.def: a stab with a pointed object
a shocking interruption of a movement, shock
thrust (single penetrating movement of the penis during sex.)
a chick (i.e. an attractive young woman)

stoot.e: From Middle Dutch stôot, from Old Dutch *stōt, from Proto-West Germanic *stauti, from Proto-Germanic *stautiz. Equivalent to a deverbal from stoten.

sterven.e: From Middle Dutch sterven, from Old Dutch stervan, from Proto-Germanic *sterbaną, itself either from Proto-Indo-European *(s)terp- (“to lose force; lose sensibility, become numb; be dead, be motionless”) or from *sterbʰ- (“to be stiff, become stiff”).

sluipen.e: From Middle Dutch slupen, from Old Dutch slūpan, from Proto-West Germanic *sleupan, from Proto-Germanic *sleupaną.

s.def: a village in Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

spui.e:
Attested as ter Zouter Speye in 1549. Derived from spui (“lift-lock”). Named after a former lock.
See also Zealandic Spuuje.

schap.e: From Middle Dutch schap, from Old Dutch *skap, from Proto-West Germanic *skap, from Proto-Germanic *skapą.

examples: 1992, A. F. Th. van der Heijden, Weerborstels, Em. Querido's Uitgeverij, page 36

scheiding.e: From scheiden + -ing. Cognate to English shedding.

s.def: a short stroke (e.g. of the pen)

streep.e: From Middle Dutch strepe, from Middle Low German strepe, from Proto-Germanic *stripō(n) or *straipō(n), of uncertain ultimate origin, perhaps derived from a lost strong verb *strīpaną, with no clear cognates outside of Germanic except for Irish sríab (“line, stripe”).

s.def: any equivalent rind on other plants
a similar exterior tissue or layer, peel or crust, e.g. on an organ
one's or something's appearance; a cover
another rough and/or protective exterior or ice

schors.e: From Middle Dutch scorce, from Old French escorce (modern écorce), itself from Medieval Latin scorcia, from Latin scortea.

s.def: A highly pejorative term for a woman.
street whore, streetwalker (a prostitute who operates in public areas)

souter.e:
From Middle Dutch souter (which existed alongside forms such as psalter and seltre), possibly with L-vocalization from Old Dutch psaltere, psaltare, from Latin psalterium (possibly by way of a Proto-West Germanic *psalterī), ultimately from Ancient Greek ψᾰλτήρῐον (psăltḗrĭon).
Alternatively, the...

stenen.e: From Old Dutch *stēnin, from Proto-Germanic *stainīnaz, equivalent to steen + -en.

staven.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

s.def: a hamlet in Oirschot, North Brabant, Netherlands

straten.e: First attested as straten in 1312-1350. Derived from the dative plural form of straat (“way, road”).

steeg.e: From Middle Dutch steech, stege, from Old Dutch steyga, *stega (“stairway”), from Proto-Germanic *stigǭ (“staircase; ladder”). Cognate with Middle Low German stēge, Old High German stega. More distantly related to Old High German steiga (from Proto-Germanic *staigō-) and German Steg (from Proto-G...

s.def: a hamlet in Asten, North Brabant, Netherlands

stegen.e: First attested as De Steege in 1838-1857. Derived from an inflected form of steeg (“alley, path, narrow road”).

s.def: a saxophone, metallic wind instrument with keys
slag (the impurities which result and are separated out when melting a metal or refining it from its ore)
hard, molten leftovers of burnt coal and other solid fuels

slak.e: From Middle Dutch slecke, slacke, from Old Dutch *slecco, from Proto-Germanic *slikkô, perhaps ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *sliǵ-ōn, from *sley- (“smooth; slick; sticky; slimy”). Cognate with Luxembourgish Schleek, German Low German Slacke, Slack (“snail”).

satirisch.e: Borrowed from German satirisch. Equivalent to satire + -isch.

s.def: to tear into strips, notably between one's fingers
to practice striptease, to remove one's clothes ostentatiously, as an erotic performance
to perform a membrane sweep, done to induce labor in a pregnant woman

examples: Spiegeltje, spiegeltje aan de wand, wie is de schoonste van het land?

spiegel.e: From Middle Dutch spiegel, from Old Dutch spēgal, from Proto-West Germanic *spēgl.

s.def: a type of spongy sweets, made of albumen and sugar (as a countable object always diminutive)

schuim.e: From Middle Dutch schuum, schum, scum, from Old Dutch *skūm, from Frankish *skūm, from Proto-Germanic *skūmaz. Cognate with German Schaum, English scum.

schapen.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

stammen.e: From stam + -en, with influence from German stammen.

s.def: a silene, any plant of the genus Silene

strijdbaar.e: From Middle Dutch strijtbaer. Equivalent to strijden + -baar.

stromen.e: From Middle Dutch strômen. Equivalent to stroom + -en.

s.def: to stretch, to extend, to reach
examples: Hun macht strekte tot het Zwarte Woud.

s.def: to reach, to extend, to stretch
examples: Hij strekte zijn arm en vervolgens zijn been.
De overeenkomst strekt tot voordeel van beide partijen.
Uw vriendelijke inleiding strekt tot eer.
Dat je dat zegt, strekt mij tot eer.

s.def: to suffice, to last, to serve, to accommodate
examples: Deze aanbieding geldt zolang de voorraad strekt.

strekken.e: From Middle Dutch strecken, from Old Dutch *strecken, from Proto-West Germanic *strakkjan. Equivalent to strak (“straight, tight, tense”) + -en (“deadjectival”) and shows the effect of historical umlaut.

s.def: a woman with little or no noticeable bosom
patron, regular customer, especially to a pub

staren.e: From Middle Dutch stāren, from Old Dutch *staron, from Proto-West Germanic *starēn.

stampen.e: From Middle Dutch stampen, from Old Dutch *stampon, from Proto-West Germanic *stampōn, from Proto-Germanic *stampōną.

s.def: span (distance between extended thumb and little finger)

storten.e: From Middle Dutch sterten, from Old Dutch *sturten, from Proto-West Germanic *sturtijan. Doublet with starten, which came from English start.

s.def: speechless, at a loss for words

sacristie.e: From Middle Dutch sacristie, from Medieval Latin sacristia.

stopcontact.e: From stoppen (“to put, to plug”) + contact.

examples: Vanwege de exorbitant hoge schulden die hij bij haar had, besloot Annie de Nigeriaanse prins te gijzelen.

s.def: blame, fault (responsibility for a mishap or mistake)
examples: Ze legde de schuld voor de misdaad bij de wanbetaler.
Maar het hof was overduidelijk over haar schuld voor.
Door mijn schuld, door mijn schuld, door mijn grote schuld!

schuld.e: From Middle Dutch schult, also schout, from Old Dutch scult, from Proto-West Germanic *skuldi, from Proto-Germanic *skuldiz.

s.def: a guilty person, an offender, a perpetrator

schuldige.e: From Middle Dutch schuldige. By surface analysis, schuldig + -e.

sorus.e: From Latin sorus, from Ancient Greek σωρός (sōrós).

sneeuwwit.e: From Middle Dutch sneewit, from Old Dutch *snēwīt, from Proto-Germanic *snaiwahwītaz. Cognate with English snow-white.

s.def: the Sound (strait that separates Zealand from Scania)

sont.e: From sont, an archaic by-form of zond (“sound, strait”).

sluik.e: From Middle Dutch sluuc (“limp”), of uncertain origin. Possibly related to English slouch, which could be from Proto-Germanic *slakaz (“slack, weak”); or otherwise *slūm- (“slack, flabby”). Also compare sluimeren (“to slumber”) and the root of German schlau.

s.def: inflection of specialer, the comparative degree of speciaal:
inflection of specialer, the comparative degree of speciaal:
inflection of specialer, the comparative degree of speciaal:
inflection of speciaalst, the superlative degree of speciaal:
inflection of speciaalst, the superlative degree of speciaal:
inflection of speciaalst, the superlative degree of speciaal:

schraal.e:
From Middle Dutch schra (“dry; poor, scanty”), either from Proto-Germanic *skrēla- or Proto-Germanic *skrēha-. Further origin uncertain.
Doublet of schriel, schrao (dialectal), cognates include West Frisian skriel, German schrāh (dialectal), Middle Low German schrā (“poor, scanty, miserable”), Ol...

stuwen.e: From Middle Dutch stouwen, ultimately from the root of stuw (“weir”). Cognate with stouwen.

stuw.e: From Middle Dutch stouwe, from Old Dutch *stōa, *stuoa, from Proto-West Germanic *stōu.

examples: Het is een spannend verhaal.

sommige.e:
From Middle Dutch sommich, somich, from som (“any, some”) + -ich. The simplex som derives from Old Dutch sum (“any, some”), from Proto-West Germanic *sum (“some”). It is found also in other combinations such as soms and somtijds.
Related to English some.

sleg.e: From Middle Dutch slegge, from Old Dutch *slegga, from Proto-Germanic *slagjǭ.

s.def: a player, someone who plays a game
a player, someone who plays a musical instrument

speler.e: From Middle Dutch speler. Equivalent to spelen + -er.

speeltje.e:
From spel (“game”) + -tje with lengthening of the root vowel as in glaasje etc. Doublet of spelletje. Compare the plurals spelen and spellen, which also show a secondary semantic distinction.
In the sense “toy” perhaps in part a new derivation from the related verb spelen (“to play”).

schijnheilig.e: 1557, from schijnen (“to seem, appear”) + heilig (“holy, sainted”).

examples: Jij bent de rots waar ik op strandde.

s.def: Solomon (Biblical figure, historical king of Israel and Judah)

salomo.e: Borrowed from Latin Salomōn. Doublet of Sjloume.

s.def: talkative (tending to talk or speak freely or often), talksome

systematisch.e: Borrowed from German systematisch. By surface analysis, systeem + -atisch.

sikkel.e: From Middle Dutch sickele, sēkele, from Old Dutch *sikila, from Proto-West Germanic *sikilu.

scheet.e: From Middle Dutch schete. Ablaut formation from schijten.

sleutelkind.e: From sleutel + kind, probably a loose calque of English latchkey child.

s.def: shive, i.e. fragment of the woody core of flax or hemp

scheef.e:
From Middle Dutch scheyff, from Proto-West Germanic *skēf, from Proto-West Germanic *skaib, from Proto-Germanic *skaibaz ‘crooked, askew’, from Proto-Indo-European *skeh₂iwo-.
Compare Latin scaevus (“left, clumsy”), Ancient Greek σκαιός (skaiós, “rude; brusque”), Latvian šķìbs (“crooked”). Cognat...

sieraad.e: Borrowed in early modern times from German Zierat (“ornament, decoration”) (modern spelling Zierrat).

s.def: a rich (varied, rare, large …) collection or find (regardless of intrinsic value)
honey, darling, sweet person (term of endearment)

schat.e: From Middle Dutch schat, from Old Dutch *skat, from Proto-West Germanic *skatt, from Proto-Germanic *skattaz.

splijten.e: From Middle Dutch spliten, from Old Dutch *splītan, from Proto-West Germanic *splītan, from Proto-Germanic *splītaną. Compare German spleißen, West Frisian and Danish splitte, English split (borrowed from Middle Dutch).

s.def: poultry, white meat, the whitish meat of fowl
a village in Midden-Drenthe, Drenthe, Netherlands

spier.e: From Middle Dutch spier (“pointed object; blade of grass; long pole; muscle”), from Old Dutch *spīr, from Proto-Germanic *spīraz, *spīrǭ. In Middle Dutch, the word took the meaning "muscle", likely due to the similarities in shape between its earlier meanings and muscle fibers.

schatkamer.e: From schat + kamer. Cognate with German Schatzkammer.

s.def: to cut corners, to use a shortcut

smokkelen.e: A frequentative derivation of Middle Dutch smuken (“to act sneakily”) with the suffix -elen from a base verb Proto-Germanic *smukkōną; compare besmuikt, smuigen.

s.def: to stream, to wash, to flow

spoelen.e: From Middle Dutch spoelen, from Old Dutch *spuolen, from Proto-West Germanic *spōlijan.

s.def: to befit, to be appropriate for
examples: Het boetekleed siert de mens.

s.def: to be nice of, to be decent of
examples: Dat siert u.

sieren.e: From Middle Dutch cieren, tsieren, sieren, borrowed from Middle High German zieren. Cognate with German zieren and the inherited form tieren.

examples: een harde schijf: a computer hard disk
een schijfje citroen: a slice of lemon

s.def: a village in Rucphen, North Brabant, Netherlands

schijf.e: From Middle Dutch schîve, from Old Dutch *skīva, from Proto-Germanic *skībǭ. Cognate with German Scheibe, English shive, Old Norse skífa.

sloom.e:
Uncertain; first attested in the late nineteenth century. Ostensibly a variant of loom (“(pleasantly) lazy”), perhaps influenced by words pertaining to slowness and sluggishness with initial sl- such as slungel, slenteren, slak and sloffen.
Note however the fixed expression slome duikelaar, which...

syntaxis.e: From Latin syntaxis, from Ancient Greek σῠ́ντᾰξῐς (sŭ́ntăxĭs).

examples: Gisteren ontdekte hij dat de spuit van de tuinslang kapot is.
Dit geneesmiddel wordt door middel van een spuit toegediend.
De guit krijgt een spuit.

s.def: euthanasia; lethal injection (particularly in relation to animals, usage in relation to humans may be considered crass)
examples: Ons konijn heeft vorige week een spuitje gekregen.
De veteraan uit de Eerste Wereldoorlog had zijn oude spuit aan de mantel gehangen.

spuit.e: From Middle Dutch spoyte, probably related to spuien.

s.def: dish particular to a certain region
examples: Dit restaurant serveert vooral streekgerechten.

s.def: Seoul (the capital city of South Korea)

snappen.e: From Middle Dutch snappen, of a group of words pertaining to snuiven (“to sniff”), referring to jerky motions or breathing. Similar sense development is found in German schnappen (“to seize, grasp, breathe”).

s.def: to hit with an arrow or similar weapon

stralen.e: From Middle Dutch strâlen. Equivalent to straal + -en.

speeksel.e:
From Middle Dutch speecsel, formed from older spekel by analogy with terms suffixed with -sel. The form spekel is from Old Dutch *spēkila, from Proto-Germanic *spaiklō. From the same root as spuwen.
Cognate with German Speichel.

s.def: shipwreck (event where a ship sinks or runs aground)

schipbreuk.e: From Middle Dutch schipbrēke, scipbrāke, scipbrēkinghe, scipbrōkinghe, equivalent to schip (“ship”) + breuk (“break, fracture”). Cognate with Middle Low German schipbrōke (“shipwreck”), Old English scipbroc, scipġebroc (“shipwreck”).

sneuvelen.e: From Middle Dutch snevelen. By surface analysis, sneven + -elen.

examples: Na een lange wandeling voelen mijn benen stijf aan.
Dit materiaal is stijver dan ik had verwacht.

stijf.e: From Middle Dutch stijf, from Old Dutch *stīf, from Proto-West Germanic *stīf, from Proto-Germanic *stīfaz.

sarcofaag.e: From French sarcophage, from Latin sarcophagus, from Ancient Greek σαρκοφάγος (sarkophágos, “coffin of limestone”, noun), so named from a supposed property of consuming the flesh of corpses laid in it, from σαρκοφάγος (sarkophágos, “flesh-eating, carnivorous”), from genitive σαρκός (sarkós) of σά...

spoed.e:
From Middle Dutch spoet, from Old Dutch *spuot, from Proto-West Germanic *spōdi.
The "emergency department" sense is a clipping of spoedafdeling.

examples: Die schurkenstaat onttrekt zich van internationale overeenkomsten en dreigt met oorlog.

schurkenstaat.e: From schurk (“thug, rogue, crook”) + -en- (plural-like interfix) + staat (“state”).

staakt-het-vuren.e: From staakt het vuren (“cease the firing [shooting]”, plural imperative), a calque of French cessez-le-feu.

sluw.e: Borrowed from Low German slu, probably from a Proto-Germanic *slūhaz (“sneaking, creeping”), perhaps ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *(s)leuǵ (“to crawl, slide”), if the original sense referred to sneaking and sliding. Attested since the late 18th century.

s.def: to strike, go on a strike

examples: Zo kondigde de ETA aan de gewapende strijd te staken als de Spaanse regering de soevereiniteit en het zelfbeschikkingsrecht van de Basken zou erkennen⁽ᵂᴾ⁾

staken.e: From Middle Dutch staken, derived from stake (“thin pole”), from Old Dutch *stako, from Frankish *stakō, from Proto-Germanic *stakô.

soevereiniteit.e: From Middle Dutch sovereineteit. Equivalent to soeverein + -iteit.

spreekwoord.e: From Middle Dutch spreecwort. Equivalent to spreken + woord. Compare German Sprichwort.

s.def: Saba (an island, dependent territory, and special municipality of the Netherlands in the Caribbean, in the southern part off the west coast of Venezuela)

saba.e: Possibly derived from a shortening or misreading of Spanish San Cristóbal, the original name of the island.

s.def: bull run, running of the bulls

schalie.e: From Middle Dutch schalie, from Old French escaille.

sluimeren.e: From Middle Dutch slumerende, from Old Dutch *slumen.

spuiten.e: From Middle Dutch spoiten. Probably related to spuwen and/or spuien.

slijk.e: From Middle Dutch slijc, from Old Dutch *slīk, from Proto-Germanic *slīką, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *sley- (“smooth; slick; sticky; slimy”). Compare English slick and slime.

snaar.e: From Middle Dutch snaer, from Old Dutch snara, from Proto-West Germanic *snarhā, from Proto-Germanic *snarhǭ. Compare Middle Low German snare.

examples: Ons zonnestelsel maakt deel uit van het Melkweg sterrenstelsel.
schijngestalte van de Maan

s.def: to swing, to sway side to side

examples: Dat gebouw wordt overmorgen gesloopt.
Ze hebben hem met honkbalknuppels gesloopt.
Hij was wekenlang compleet gesloopt na de marathon.

slopen.e: From Middle Dutch slôpen, from Old Dutch *slōpen, from Proto-Germanic *slaupijaną.

examples: Door haar spiraaltje heeft ze heftige krampen.

spiraal.e: Borrowed from French spirale, from Medieval Latin spiralis, from Latin spira, from Ancient Greek σπεῖρα (speîra, “wreath, coil, twist”).

samentrekken.e: From samen (“together”) + trekken (“pull”), analogous to Latin con- (“together”) + trahere (“draw”) = contract.

snoeren.e: From Middle Dutch snoeren, from Old Dutch *snuoren, from Proto-West Germanic *snōrijan, from Proto-Germanic *snōrijaną. By surface analysis, snoer (“cord”) + -en.

s.def: vernacular (everyday/colloquial speech), spoken language, everyday language
examples: In deze regio wordt een specifieke spreektaal gebruikt die afwijkt van de standaardtaal.
De docent moedigt de studenten aan om ook buiten de les de spreektaal te oefenen.

slagboom.e: From Middle Dutch slachboom. Equivalent to slag + boom.

snoezelen.e: Blend of snuffelen (“to sniff”) + doezelen (“to doze”).

snuffelen.e: An iterative of snuffen (“to snuff”). Cognate to English snuffle.

s.def: reservoir: artificial lake used as a source of water supply
a sturgeon, fish of the genus Acipenser

steur.e:
From Middle Dutch store, from Old Dutch sturo, from Proto-West Germanic *sturjō, from Proto-Germanic *sturjô (“sturgeon”).
Cognates include Old High German sturio and Old English styria and more remotely Russian осётр (osjótr, “sturgeon”), Lithuanian eršketras.

schenking.e: From Middle Dutch schenkinge. Equivalent to schenken + -ing.

staafkerk.e: Compound of staaf + kerk, a calque from either a Scandinavian language or, less probably, English.

s.def: cartoonist, comic artist (drawer of comics or cartoons)

schoeien.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch schoeyen, inherited from Old Dutch *skuoion, inherited from Proto-Germanic *skōhōną, from Proto-Germanic *skōhaz. Doublet of schoen (“shoe (footwear)”, noun).

s.def: to complete multiple tracks/variants of education at the same educational level (i.e. at secondary or tertiary education)

stapelen.e: From Middle Dutch stapelen, borrowed from Middle Low German stapelen, related to the noun stapel, from Old Saxon stapol (“basis, pillar, foundation”).

s.def: scale (keratinous segment of certain animals' skin)
pigmented, chitinous segment of butterfly wings
a similarly flaky part of the exterior of plants
Solomon Islands (a country consisting of the majority of the Solomon Islands archipelago in Melanesia, in Oceania, as well as the Santa Cruz Islands)
Solomon Islands (an archipelago in Melanesia, split between the countries of the Solomon Islands and Papua New Guinea)
Socotra (an island of Yemen, between the Guardafui Channel to the west, Gulf of Aden to the northwest, the Somali Sea to the south, and the Arabian Sea to the east)
Saint Vincent and the Grenadines (an archipelago and country in the Caribbean, comprising the islands of Saint Vincent and the island chain of the Grenadines)
power bill (bill paid for consumption of power e.g. in a household)
a medical symptom, manifestation of a syndrome
an indicator, characteristic of the presence of something else

spinnenweb.e: From Middle Dutch spinnewebbe. Equivalent to spin + -en- + web.

s.def: mother of one's boyfriend or girlfriend

schoonmoeder.e: From Middle Dutch schoonmoeder, a calque of Middle French belle-mère. By surface analysis, schoon + moeder.

s.def: lilac, woody plant of the genus Syringa

sering.e: Borrowed from New Latin syringa, from sȳrinx, from Ancient Greek σῦρῐγξ (sûrĭnx).

stroper.e: From Middle Dutch strooper. By surface analysis, stropen + -er.

stofferen.e: From Middle Dutch stofferen, from Old French estoffer, from Old High German stoffōn, from Proto-West Germanic *stoppōn. Doublet of stoppen.

s.def: main jet or pilot jet of the carburetor
a city and former municipality of De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood and former municipality of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

sloten.e:
* (Friesland) First attested as sloten in 1384. Calque of Old Frisian slāt (“ditch, dug watercourse”) in the dative plural form. See also West Frisian Sleat.
* (Noord-Holland) First attested as sloton in the first half of the 11th century. Derived from the dative plural form of the hydronym Slote...

schone kunsten.e: From schone, inflection of schoon 'beautiful' and kunsten 'arts'.

schroef.e: Borrowed from Middle French escroue, from Latin scrōfa (“female pig”) through comparison with the corkscrew shape of a pig's penis. Compare Old French escruve, from Old Dutch *scrūva ("screw"; whence Middle Dutch schruyve (“screw”)).

s.def: a shin, the front of a vertebrate leg beneath the knee

scheenbeen.e: Compound of scheen (“shin”) + been (“bone”). Compare German Schienbein, English shinbone, Danish skinneben, Swedish skenben.

s.def: metabolism (complete set of chemical reactions that occur in living cells)
a razor, a fine knife or blade for shaving
any stiffening additive, especially for putting up wallpaper
term of abuse for a coward

smeer.e: From Middle Dutch smeer, from Old Dutch smero, from Proto-Germanic *smerwą. Cognate with English smear.

s.def: to smear, to rub a substance

examples: Ik zag twee beren broodjes smeren.

s.def: to grease, to oil, etc. (to make run more smoothly)
examples: Je fiets moet nodig gesmeerd worden.

s.def: to corrupt, to pay off, to bribe
to get out, to make one's getaway, to leave
examples: Ik pak mijn koffers en ik smeer hem nu.

smeren.e: From Middle Dutch smeren, from Old Dutch *smirwen, from Proto-West Germanic *smirwijan, from Proto-Germanic *smirwijaną, from *smerwą (“grease, fat”) (modern smeer).

s.def: to grind (to frottage or dance pressed against another in a sexually suggestive way)
to bring in, notably a crop to the barn

schuren.e: From Middle Dutch schuren, probably from Old French escurer. More at English scour, a borrowing from Middle Dutch.

schuur.e: From Middle Dutch schure, from Old Dutch skūra, from Proto-Germanic *skūrō. Cognate with German Scheuer.

schromen.e: From Middle Dutch schromen. Further etymology unknown; the word is attested only from the late Middle Ages onward and is limited to Dutch, Frisian and Low German.

examples: Na het maken van een grote fout, had ze een sterk schuldgevoel en wilde ze het graag goedmaken.
Hij kon het schuldgevoel niet van zich afschudden, ook al wist hij dat het niet zijn schuld was.

s.def: the act of taking a position
the act of taking a position

stellingname.e: From stelling + name, cf. stelling nemen.

s.def: tusk, a large tooth extending outside the mouth, as on an elephant, fit as a weapon

stront.e:
From Middle Dutch stront (“shit, dreck, manure”), from Old Dutch *strunt (“excrement, turd, shit”), from Proto-West Germanic *strunt (“dirt, filth”).
Compare French étron (“turd, crap”), Italian stronzo (“turd, crap”). Cognate with English strunt.

s.def: piggy bank, pig-shaped container to save change in
stand (designated spot where someone or something may stand or wait)
examples: een busstandplaats

strateeg.e: Borrowed from French statège, from Latin stratēgus, from Ancient Greek στρᾰτηγός (strătēgós).

s.def: mural, painting (usually meant on a wall as opposed to schilderij)

spaarzaam.e: From Middle Dutch *spaersam, attested in spaersamheit. Equivalent to sparen + -zaam.

scheer.e: Borrowing, likely from a North Germanic language. Ultimately from Old Norse sker.

stabiel.e: Borrowed from Middle French stabile, from Latin stabilis.

s.def: a neighbourhood and former municipality of Haarlem, North Holland, Netherlands

schoten.e: First attested as scatan in 1125-1130. Derived from the dative plural form of Old Dutch skōta (“piece of land which juts upwards”).

s.def: the Suez Canal, linking the Egyptian Red Sea - and Mediterranean coasts

suezkanaal.e: Compound of Suez (“riverine Egyptian port city”) + kanaal (“canal”).

sculptuur.e: From Old French [Term?], from Latin sculptūra (“sculpture”), from sculpō (“to cut out, to carve in stone”).

spionage.e: Borrowed from French espionnage. By surface analysis, spioneren + -age.

sierlijk.e: From sieren (“to befit”) + -lijk. Unrelated to potsierlijk.

s.def: To rest, be based/ founded, on something (such as an argumentation)

examples: want naar de brieven te oordeelen was de Schrijfster eene autrice van den eersten rang, en toch kende hij geen enkel werk uit vrouwelijke pen verdienstelijk genoeg om aan haar te worden toegeschreven.

s.def: to fart, to waft, to trump

scheten.e: Related to scheet (“fart”) and schijten (“to shit”).

examples: Tot mijn grote spijt

spijt.e:
From Middle Dutch despijt, from Old French despit (from which also English despite), from Latin despectus (“looking down on”).
The first syllable de- was later rebracketed and interpreted as a definite article ("de spijt") and was then dropped.

symbiose.e: Borrowed from French symbiose, from Latin symbiosis.

s.def: bolt, to lock a door etc.
something shoven; a portion of sorts

schuif.e: From Middle Dutch schuuf, from schuven (“to shove”).

schoof.e: From Middle Dutch scoef, from Old Dutch *skōf, from Proto-West Germanic *skaub.

stroef.e: From Middle Dutch struuf, from Old Dutch *strūf, from Proto-Germanic *strūbaz (“stiff, rough, bristly, shaggy”), from Proto-Indo-European *strewbʰ-, *strewb- (“stiff”). Cognate with Middle Low German struuf, West Frisian stroef, German straub and struppig.

s.def: to spar, to have a practice fight
São Tomé and Príncipe (a country and archipelago of Central Africa in the Atlantic Ocean)

sao tomé en principe.e: Portuguese São Tomé (island named after the apostle Saint Thomas) + en 'and' + Portuguese Principe (island named after a Portuguese royal prince)

s.def: a symbol, sign, character, glyph or anything of symbolic value

symbool.e: Borrowed from French symbole, from Latin symbolum, from Ancient Greek σύμβολον (súmbolon). Middle Dutch used the Latin form symbolum.

s.def: slice (a piece cut off from a whole)

snee.e: From older snede with syncope of d, from Middle Dutch snede.

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

speers.e: First attested as spaers in 1481. Borrowed from West Frisian Spears, potentially derived from Old Frisian Sparre (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi-.

s.def: chemical, relating to or applying chemistry
a spoke, part between the center and outer rim of a wheel etc.

spaak.e: From Middle Dutch spake, an Ingvaeonic form of the more usual speke, from Old Dutch *spēka, from Proto-Germanic *spaikǭ, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *spey- (“sharp point”).

s.def: a bribe, corrupt payment to buy an illegitimate favor (usually used in the plural form)

steekpenning.e: From Middle Dutch steecpenninc (“bribe, alm”). Equivalent to steken + penning.

s.def: penalty, a free-kick from the penalty spot awarded to the grieved team in football etc.

schrappen.e: From Middle Dutch schrappen. Ultimately from the root of schrapen (“to scrape”).

schelvis.e: From Middle Dutch schelvisch, from Old Dutch *skelfisc. Analyzable as schel (“peel”) + vis (“fish”), the former being a dated variant of schil. The name is due either to the relatively large scales or to the layered meat of the fish. Compare German Schellfisch.

From Old Northern French scars, escars ("sparing, niggard, parsimonious, miserly, poor"; > French échars, Medieval Latin scarsus (“diminished, reduced”)), of uncertain origin. One theory is that it derives originally from a Late Latin *scarpsus, *excarpsus, a participle...

Cognates include German scheel, Old English sceolh, Old Norse skjalgr, Latin scelus (“crime”) and Ancient Greek σκολιός...

schipperskerk.e:
Compound of schipper (“skipper, sailor”) and kerk (“church”). Named after a church built in 1949.
See also Limburgish Sjipperskèrk.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Sittard-Geleen, Limburg, Netherlands

sanderbout.e: Derived from a local variant of the name of the Frankish king Zwentibold (c.870 - 900).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Sittard-Geleen, Limburg, Netherlands

stadbroek.e:
Compound of stad (“city”) and broek (“marsh, marshland”).
See also Limburgish Sjtadbrouk.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Beek, Limburg, Netherlands

spaubeek.e:
Potentially attested as spaltbeche in 1148, attested with certainty as spaltbeke in 1175. Compound of Old Dutch spalt (“gorge, vale”) (in this specifc case "gully") and beke (“brook, stream”).
See also Limburgish Sjpawbik.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Beekdaelen, Limburg, Netherlands

schimmert.e:
First attested as scinmottera and schimorta in the mid-11th century. Compound of dialectal schijn (“land cleared for agriculture”) and Latin mortarius (“muddy pool”). Compare Schinnen, Schin op Geul and Schinveld.
See also Limburgish Sjömmert.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Beekdaelen, Limburg, Netherlands

schinnen.e:
Attested with certainty as scinne in 1208. Potentially derived from the hydronym Schin (related to Old Dutch skīnan (“shine, sparkle”)) or from dialectal schijn (“land cleared for agriculture”). Compare Schimmert, Schin op Geul and Schinveld.
See also Limburgish Sjènne.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Beekdaelen, Limburg, Netherlands

schinveld.e: First attested as in uilla schinneuelt in 1180. Likely a compound of dialectal schijn (“land cleared for agriculture”) and veld (“field”). A reading of the first element as the hydronym Schin (related to Old Dutch skīnan (“shine, sparkle”)) is less likely. Compare Schimmert, Schinnen and Schin op...

s.def: a village in Beekdaelen, Limburg, Netherlands

sweikhuizen.e:
First attested as suechusen in 1280. Compound of a dialectal form of zwaag (“pasture, cattle field”) and the dative plural form of huis (“house”). Compare Schwachhausen and Schweighausen.
See also Limburgish Zjweikese, Sjweikese.

s.def: a hamlet in Beekdaelen, Limburg, Netherlands

swier.e:
First attested as scherwigen in 1246. Etymology unknown. According to one hypothesis, the toponym is derived from Latin *carrucaria (“ploughland”). Compare Baaks-Sweijer.
See also Limburgish Zjwier, Sjwier.

s.def: ski train (train that is marketed towards winter sport tourists)
a neighbourhood of Brunssum, Limburg, Netherlands

schuttersveld.e:
Derived from a compound of schutter (“militiaman, rifleman”) and veld (“field”). The neighbourhood was built on a former training ground of the local militia.
See also Limburgish Sjuttersjveld.

s.def: a neighbourhood and former municipality of Landgraaf, Limburg, Netherlands

schaesberg.e: First attested as Gaesberg in the 16th century. Compound of an unclear first element and berg (“hill, elevated place”). spekholzerheide.e:
First attested as Speckholzeheide in 1614. Compound of the toponym Spekholz and heide (“heath, heathland”).
See also Central Franconian Sjpekhei.

s.def: a hamlet in Heerlen, Limburg, Netherlands

schurenberg.e: First attested as Schuyrenberg in 1803-1820. Compound of the toponym Terschuren and berg (“hill, elevated place”).

schandelen.e:
First attested as schandelen in 1415. Etymology unknown. Perhaps a compound of schoon (“clean, free of weeds”) and either lo (“light forest”) or laar (“intensively cultivated forest”). Compare Schandelo and Schoonloo.
See also Limburgish Sjandele.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Heerlen, Limburg, Netherlands

schuureik.e: First attested as Schuyreyk in 1803-1820. Compound of the toponym Terschuren and eik (“oak, Quercus sp.”).

schaesbergerveld.e: Compound of the toponym Schaesberg and veld (“field”).

schin op geul.e:
First attested as de schina in the mid-11th century. Of uncertain etymology. Perhaps derived from the hydronym *Skinion or from schijn (“land cleared for agriculture”) with op (“on”) and the hydronym Geul added to aid identification. Compare Schimmert, Schinnen and Schinveld.
See also Limburgish ...

s.def: a hamlet in Valkenburg aan de Geul, Limburg, Netherlands

schoonbron.e:
First attested as sconenburne in 1294-1297. Derived from a compound of schoon (“clean, pure”) and bron (“spring, source”).
See also Limburgish Sjuèmer.

s.def: a hamlet in Valkenburg aan de Geul, Limburg, Netherlands

sint gerlach.e: First attested as prope Sanctum Gerlacum in 1217. Named after Saint Gerlach.

strabeek.e:
First attested as stroebach in 1458. Potentially a compound of stro (“straw”) and beek (“brook, stream”).
See also Limburgish Sjtraobaek.

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of Valkenburg aan de Geul, Limburg, Netherlands

strucht.e:
First attested as Strucht in 1603. Derived from Middle Dutch struch (“bush”) with collectivising suffix -t.
See also Limburgish Sjtröch.

stoepert.e:
First attested as Stoepert in 1913. Derived from stoep (“sidewalk, pavement”). Compare Plenkert.
See also Limburgish De Sjtoepert.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Gulpen-Wittem, Limburg, Netherlands

slenaken.e:
First attested as sledenake in 1253. Derived from Proto-Germanic *Slado (a personal name) suffixed with Gallo-Roman Latin -iniacas. Not derived from Salinius or *slede (“slope, slippery place”). Compare Schleidweiler.
See also Limburgish Sjlennich.

s.def: a hamlet in Gulpen-Wittem, Limburg, Netherlands

schweiberg.e:
First attested as Scheveyberch in 1537. Compound derived from Limburgish schevei (“hollow road”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”). Compare Chevremont and Kaffeberg.
See also Limburgish Sjweeberig.

s.def: a hamlet in Gulpen-Wittem, Limburg, Netherlands

sinselbeek.e: First attested as Sinselbach in 1899. Named after a nearby stream.

s.def: a hamlet in Gulpen-Wittem, Limburg, Netherlands

stokhem.e:
First attested as stochem in 1260. Compound of stok (“stick”) and heem (“home, settlement”). Compare Houthem, Stokkem and Stokkum.
See also Limburgish Sjtókkem.

s.def: a village in Eijsden-Margraten, Limburg, Netherlands

scheulder.e:
First attested as scoler in 1421. Derived from schuilen (“hide, take shelter”).
See also Limburgish Sjuuëlder.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Eijsden-Margraten, Limburg, Netherlands

sint geertruid.e:
First attested as in monte in 1178. Named after Saint Gertrude of Nivelles.

s.def: a hamlet in Eijsden-Margraten, Limburg, Netherlands

schey.e: First attested as Schey in 1803-1820. Derived from scheide (“watershed, border, hill ridge”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Maastricht, Limburg, Netherlands

sint maartenspoort.e: First attested as sent Martijns port te wijc in 1400. Derived from a compound of the name of Saint Martin of Tours and poort (“gate”). The neighbourhood was named after a gate which stood just north of a church dedicated to Saint Martin.

a neighbourhood and former municipality of Maastricht, Limburg, Netherlands

sint pieter.e:
* (Zeeland) Attested as St. Pieter in 1866. Named after Saint Peter. Allegedly named after a former Spanish fortress, although the hamlet may actually be named after a former church dedicated to Saint Peter.
* (Limburg) First attested as Sanctum Petrum in 1155. Named after Saint Peter. Formerly a...

scharn.e:
Potentially attested as apud scharnen in 1151, attested with certainty as in scarnia in 1170-1180. Perhaps related to West Frisian skarne (“manure”), Old English scearn (“manure”). Formerly an independent hamlet.
See also Limburgish Sjaan.

s.def: a hamlet in Meerssen, Limburg, Netherlands

schietecoven.e:
First attested as Schitekoven in 1501. Etymology uncertain. Perhaps derived from Old Dutch *Scite (a personal name) and the dative plural form of hof (“farmstead, homestead”). However, there are no known cognates of the name *Scite in Limburg. Compare Schettens.
See also Limburgish Sjietekove.

s.def: a hamlet in Meerssen, Limburg, Netherlands

snijdersberg.e:
First attested as Sniedesberg in 1803-1820. Derived from a compound of Snijders (a surname) and berg (“hill, elevated place”).
See also Limburgish Sniedersberg.

a village and former municipality of Maasgouw, Limburg, Netherlands

stevensweert.e:
First attested as werde in 1221. Derived from a compound of the name of Saint Stephen and weert (“land next to water, riverine island”).

s.def: a hamlet in Maasgouw, Limburg, Netherlands

santfort.e: First attested as Zandfort in 1835. Compound of zand (“sand”) and voorde (“ford, crossing”). Doublet of Zandvoort and Zandfort.

schaapsbrug.e:
First attested as Schapsbruggen in 1646. Derived from a compound of schaap (“sheep”) and brug (“bridge”). Named after a bridge near the hamlet.

s.def: a hamlet in Leudal, Limburg, Netherlands
a hamlet in Leudal, Limburg, Netherlands
a hamlet in Leudal, Limburg, Netherlands

strubben.e:
First attested as Streube in 1838-1857. Derived from dialectal strobbe (“stumps of trees and shrubs remaining after a forest has been cleared”). Compare Stobben and Stramproy.

stramproy.e:
First attested as stramprode in 1299. Compound of dialectal stramp (“stump of a tree or branch with one or more branches”) and rode (“land cleared of trees”). Compare Stobben and Strubben.

swartbroek.e:
Potentially attested as Swartbroeck in the 16th century, attested with certainty as Swaerbroick around 1600. Derived from a compound of zwart (“black”) and broek (“marsh, marshland”).

strateris.e: First attested as Strateris in 1826. Potentially a contracted compound of straat (“street”) or an otherwise unknown toponym Straat and huis (“house”). Compare Lankes, Noenes and Oosterens.

schafelt.e: First attested as Schaefeld in 1899. Perhaps a compound of schaap (“sheep”) and veld (“field”).

schoorveld.e: First attested as Schoorveld in 1838-1857. Derived from a compound of schoor (“bank of a small river”) and veld (“field”).

soeterbeek.e: First attested as soeterbeecke in 1406. Derived from a nearby hydronym.

spiesberg.e: First attested as Maris Berg in 1838-1857. Compound of spies (“spike”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”).

spurkt.e: First attested as Spurkt in 1899. Derived from dialectal spurk (“alder buckthorn, Frangula alnus”) with collectivising suffix -t.

someren-eind.e: First attested as het Eind in 1838-1857. Compound of the toponym Someren and eind (“edge, end”).

First attested as zurendonc in 1307. Compound of either a term cognate to zuur (“sour, acidic”) or to Old English sure (“dock, Rumex sp.”) and donk (“sandy hill protruding above a swamp”).

sterksel.e: First attested as sterkesele and sterxele in 1196-1198. Compound of either sterk (“strong, powerful”) or sterke (“young cow”) and Old Dutch sele (“hall home”). Compare Peursum, Poederoijen, Poeijers, Vaarle, Vaarsel, Varsen, Varssel, Varsselder, Varsseveld, Ten Vorsel and Bolsele.
