sintenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of Sint (“saint”) and buurt (“neighbourhood”). The neighbourhood was named for its many streets named after saints.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands

schuttersbosch.e: Derived from a compound of schutter (“militiaman”) and bos (“forest”). The name refers to a forest that was used as a training area by the Eindhoven militia.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands

spaaihoef.e: First attested as De Spaaihoef in 1866. Likely derived from spade (“spade, shovel”) and hoeve (“farmstead”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands

schouwbroek.e: First attested as scautbroec in 1340. Derived from a compound of dialectal schouw (“pasture, hayfield”) and broek (“marsh, marshland”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Oosterhout, North Brabant, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Haarlem, North Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Deventer, Overijssel, Netherlands

schrijversbuurt.e: All compounds of the plural form of schrijver (“writer, author”) and buurt (“neighbourhood”). The neighbourhoods' streets are named after famous authors.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Geldrop-Mierlo, North Brabant, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Helmond, North Brabant, Netherlands

stiphout.e: First attested as stilpot in 1155. Compound of stip (“pallisade”) and hout (“rising deciduous forest”). Compare Stepelo, Stipdonk and Stiepel.

s.def: a hamlet in Helmond, North Brabant, Netherlands

scheepstal.e: First attested as schepstal in 1246. Compound of Middle Dutch scheep (“ship, boat”) and stal (“anchorage, safe haven”). Compare Aarle-Rixtel and Boxtel.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Helmond, North Brabant, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Meierijstad, North Brabant, Netherlands

schijndel.e: First attested as scinlre mere just before 1233. Compound of an unclear first element and lo (“light forest”). The first element may be cognate with Old Frisian skene (“scurf, dandruff”) and dialectal schin (“dandruff”). A relationship with schijnen (“shine, glitter”) is less likely.

s.def: a city and former municipality of Meierijstad, North Brabant, Netherlands

sint-oedenrode.e: First attested as A. Rodae Comitis in the 12th century. Compound of Sint (“saint”), Oda (a personal name) and rode (“land cleared of trees”). Named after Saint Oda of Scotland, who fled from Scotland to Brabant and became a hermit, reportedly somewhere near the future city.

s.def: a village and municipality of North Brabant, Netherlands

sint-michielsgestel.e: Potentially attested as gestele around 1173, attested with certainty as gestele in 1305. Compound of the name of Saint Michael and the common toponymic element gastel (“inn, guesthouse”). Named after a local church dedicated to the saint.

s.def: a hamlet in 's-Hertogenbosch, North Brabant, Netherlands

schotsheuvel.e: First attested as Schotsheuvel in 1673. Compound of schoot (“piece of land that juts upward”) or schot (“enclosed area, fenced-in land”) and heuvel (“hill”).

s.def: a village in Altena, North Brabant, Netherlands

sleeuwijk.e: First attested as slewijck in 1268. Compound of dialectal slee (“blunt, sour”) or slee (“blackthorn, Prunus spinosa”) and wijk (“village, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Altena, North Brabant, Netherlands

stenenheul.e: First attested as Steene Heul in 1838-1857. Compound of steen (“stone”) and heul (“floodgate with an arched bridge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Altena, North Brabant, Netherlands

steurgat.e: First attested as het Steurgat in 1729-1730. Compound of steur (“sturgeon (Acipenser sturio)”) and gat (“mouth, estuary”). Originally a hydronym.

s.def: a hamlet in Drimmelen, North Brabant, Netherlands

steelhoven.e: First attested as sthenlo in 1213. Compound of steen (“stone”) and lo (“light forest”). The second element was eventually replaced by the plural form of hof (“farmstead, homestead”).

s.def: a hamlet in Drimmelen, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Zundert, North Brabant, Netherlands

stuivezand.e:
* (Drimmelen) First attested as stuvensant in 1460. Derived from Middle Dutch stuufsant (“drift-sand, land covered in drift-sand”).
* (Zundert) First attested as in Stuffesande in 1544. Derived from Middle Dutch stuufsant (“drift-sand, land covered in drift-sand”).

s.def: a hamlet in Oosterhout, North Brabant, Netherlands

seters.e: First attested as ceters in 1287. Etymology unknown. Not related to Sittard.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Hoeksche Waard, South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Oosterhout, North Brabant, Netherlands

strijen.e:
* (Zuid-Holland) First attested as strinen in 1167. Derived from a hydronym, derived in turn from Proto-Indo-European *stri (“strike, hit”) or *sreu (“flow”). An alternative hypothesis proposes a derivation from (a term cognate to) struinen (“wander about, amble about”).
* (Noord-Brabant) First a...

s.def: a hamlet in Geertruidenberg, North Brabant, Netherlands

statendam.e: First attested as Statendam Voet Veer in 1838-1857. Compound of Staten and dam (“dam”). Originally the name of a dam constructed by the Estates of Holland.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Geertruidenberg, North Brabant, Netherlands

sandoel.e: First attested as t Santdeel in 1753. Compound of zand (“sand”) and doel (“ditch serving as a boundary between two fields”). Compare Den Dool and Kadoelen.

s.def: a hamlet in Waalwijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

sprangsevaart.e: First attested as SPRANGSCHE-VAART in 1847. Compound of the toponym Sprang (now part of Sprang-Capelle) and vaart (“canal, excavated watercourse”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Waalwijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

sprang-capelle.e: Sprang is first attested as spranc in 1331. Capelle is first attested as capella in 1257. Sprang is derived from (now dialectal) sprang (“spring of a brook or stream”). Capelle is derived from Middle Dutch capelle (“chapel”). Formerly two independent villages.

s.def: a hamlet in Tilburg, North Brabant, Netherlands

schoorstraat.e: First attested as scoerstraet in 1430. Potentially a compound of schoor (“small bridge”) and straat (“street, road”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Tilburg, North Brabant, Netherlands

stokhasselt.e: First attested as op die stoc hasselt in 1398. Compound of an unclear first element Middle Dutch stoc and the toponym Hasselt. It is not clear whether stok here refers to a chapel or a small section of cleared forest. Compare Quirijnstok and Stokske.

s.def: a hamlet in Goirle, North Brabant, Netherlands

spaansehoek.e: First attested as Spaansche hoek in 1899. Compound of Spaans (“Spanish”) and hoek (“corner, isolated place”). The toponym may contain a reference to the presence of Spanish troops near the hamlet during the Eighty Years' War.

s.def: a hamlet in Baarle-Nassau, North Brabant, Netherlands

schaluinen.e: First attested as schalluynen in 1479. Named after Ascalon. Compare Schelluinen.

s.def: a village in Alphen-Chaam, North Brabant, Netherlands

strijbeek.e: First attested as strijbeeck in 1256. Derived from a hydronym related to the ancient hydronym Striene.

s.def: a hamlet in Alphen-Chaam, North Brabant, Netherlands

snijders-chaam.e: First attested as snijderscaem in 1456. Compound of Snijders (a surname) and the toponym Chaam.

s.def: a village in Rucphen, North Brabant, Netherlands

st. willebrord.e: First attested as het Heideke in 1536. Named after Saint Willibrord.

s.def: a village in Rucphen, North Brabant, Netherlands

sprundel.e: First attested with certainty as de sprundele in 1282. Etymology unknown. Proposed etymology include a compound of Old Dutch *sprund (“lively, vibrant”) and lo (“light forest”) or a derivation from a term cognate to Old English sprindil (“glue used to trap birds, bird trap”).

s.def: a former municipality of North Brabant, Netherlands

sprundel en vorenseinde.e: Named after Sprundel and the former fiefdom Vorenseinde.

s.def: a hamlet in Rucphen, North Brabant, Netherlands

steenpaal.e: First attested as steenpael in 1490. Compound of steen (“stone”) and paal (“pole, marker”). The hamlet sprung up close to a boundary marker.

s.def: a village in Halderberge, North Brabant, Netherlands

stampersgat.e: First attested as Stoutersgat in 1639. Compound of Stamper (a surname) and gat (“mouth of a creek”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Moerdijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

standdaarbuiten.e: First attested as tgors standerbuyten in 1461. Compound of Middle Dutch standaert (“boundary marker”) and buiten (“outside of”).

s.def: a hamlet in Moerdijk, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

strooiendorp.e:
* (Noord-Brabant) First attested as Strooijen Dorp in 1838-1857. Compound of strooien (“made of straw”) and dorp (“village”). Named for the thatched-roof huts that made up the initial settlement. Compare Stroobos, Stroodorp, Stroodorpe and Strooienstad.
* (Overijssel) First attested as Strooiendo...

s.def: a hamlet in Moerdijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

stadse dijk.e: First attested as De Stadsche Dijk in 1856-1857. Derived from the toponym Willemstad and dijk (“levee”).

s.def: a hamlet in Bergen op Zoom, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

slikkenburg.e:
* (Noord-Brabant) Attested as Slykenburch in 1622. Compound of the plural form of slik (“clay-like alluvial land which has not been impoldered yet”) and burg (“reinforced settlement, castle”). Named after a former defensive emplacement.
* (Zeeland) Attested as Slyckenborg in 1617. Compound of the...

s.def: a hamlet in Bergen op Zoom, North Brabant, Netherlands

spinolaberg.e: First attested as Spinolaberg in 1869. Derived from Spanish Spinola (a surname) and berg (“hill, elevated place”). Named after Ambrogio Spinola (1569-1630), a military commander employed by the Spanish Empire who led a failed attack on Bergen op Zoom in 1622.

s.def: a hamlet in Woensdrecht, North Brabant, Netherlands

schoelieberg.e: First attested as Schoelieberg in 1838-1857. Compound of dialectal schoelie (“baker's peel, scoundrel”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”).

s.def: a city and former municipality of Tholen, Zeeland, Netherlands

sint-maartensdijk.e:
First attested as agger Sancti Martini in 1281. Compound of the name of Saint Martin of Tours and dijk (“levee”).
See also Zealandic Smerdiek.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Tholen, Zeeland, Netherlands

scherpenisse.e:
First attested as scarpenes in 1204. Compound of either Middle Dutch scharp (“sharp, pointed”) or Scarpe (a personal name) and nesse (“headland, spit”).
See also Zealandic Schaerpenisse.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Tholen, Zeeland, Netherlands

sint-annaland.e: First attested as sint annenlant in 1493. Compound of the name of Saint Anne and land (“land”). The name was given as a reference to the namesake of Anne of Burgundy (ca. 1435-1508), who initiated the land-reclamation project which resulted in the creation of the polders which the settlement was ...

s.def: A village, peninsula, former island and former municipality now belonging to the municipality of Tholen in the province of Zeeland, The Netherlands.

sint philipsland.e: First attested as ecclesia sti Philippi Apostoli, St. Philipskerc in 1490-1495. Compound of the name of Saint Philip the Apostle and land (“land”). Named after a church built by Anna of Burgundy (ca. 1435-1508), an illegitimate daughter of Philip of Burgundy; the church was dedicated to Saint Phi...

s.def: a village and former municipality of Tholen, Zeeland, Netherlands

stavenisse.e:
First attested as stauenesse in 1206. Compound of Middle Dutch stave (“stick, wooden pole”) and nesse (“headland, spit”). Compare Spijkenisse, Staverden, Stavoren and Stavern.
See also Zealandic Staevenisse.

s.def: a hamlet in Tholen, Zeeland, Netherlands

schoondorp.e: Attested as Schoondorpe around 1750. Compound of schoon (“clean, free of weeds”) and dorp (“village, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Tholen, Zeeland, Netherlands

steenenkruis.e: Attested as Steenekruis in 1838-1857. Compound of steen (“stone”) and kruis (“cross”). Named after a memorial cross located near the settlement.

s.def: a hamlet in Tholen, Zeeland, Netherlands

strijenham.e: Attested as Stryenham around 1750. Compound of the hydronym Striene and ham (“alluvial land”). See Strijen.

s.def: a municipality and island of Zeeland, Netherlands

schouwen-duiveland.e: Named after the former islands of Schouwen and Duiveland, which were joined to from the larger island of Schouwen-Duiveland.

s.def: a village in Schouwen-Duiveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

scharendijke.e:
First attested as schaerdendijke in 1487. Compound of Middle Dutch schaerde (“tear, deep gully”) and dijk (“levee”).
See also Zealandic Schaerendieke.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Schouwen-Duiveland, Zeeland, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands

serooskerke.e:
* (Schouwen-Duiveland) First attested as Ecclesia Alardi in 1395-1396. Compound of a contracted version of des heer (“of the lord”), Old Dutch Alard (a personal name) and kerk (“church”). See also Zealandic Sêêskaarke, Sêêskerke, Seêskerke.
* (Veere) First attested as alerdeskirkam, alerdeskirke ...

s.def: a village and former municipality of Schouwen-Duiveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

sirjansland.e:
First attested as Zeer Jans lant in 1573. Contracted compound of heer (“lord”), Jan (a personal name) and land (“land”). The name references Jan of Beaumont (ca. 1288-1356), who initiated the land reclamation project which the later settlement would be built on.
See also Zealandic Sturjalland, Sir.

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Schouwen-Duiveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

schuddebeurs.e:
* (Hulst) Attested as SCHUDDEBEURS in 1847. Compound of schudden (“shake, jangle”) and beurs (“purse”). Apparently named after a former inn.
* (Schouwen-Duiveland) Attested as Schuddeburs around 1750. Compound of schudden (“shake, jangle”) and beurs (“purse”). Named after an inn or, according to ...

s.def: a hamlet in Noord-Beveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

stroodorp.e: Attested as STROOIJEDORP (HET) in 1847. Compound of stro (“straw”) and dorp (“village, settlement”). Named for the fact that the first houses of the settlement were constructed with straw roofs, as well as to contrast with nearby Plankendorp. Compare Stroobos, Stroodorpe, Strooiendorp and Strooie...

s.def: a hamlet in Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands

sint jan ten heere.e:
Attested as Kerckwerue in 1560. Derived from the name of Saint John the Baptist. Named after a former commandery of the Knights Hospitaller.
See also Zealandic Sint Jan t'n Éere.

s.def: a hamlet in Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands

sint janskerke.e: First attested as Sancti Iohannis Ecclesiam in 1235. Compound of the name of Saint John and kerk (“church”).

s.def: a hamlet in Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Middelburg, Zeeland, Netherlands

sint laurens.e:
First attested as ecclesia sancti Laurentii alias Popkinsburgh in the 13th century. Named after Saint Lawrence of Rome.
See also Zealandic Sint-Lauwers.

s.def: a hamlet in Middelburg, Zeeland, Netherlands

schellach.e: First attested as scellagt in 1270 or 1273. Derived from Middle Dutch schilacht (“dispute, argument”). The toponym refers to a border area which was the subject of land disputes. Compare Cabauw, Hebrecht, Kijfhoek, Kijfwaard, Kibbelgaarn, Kibbelveen, Krakeel, Krakeelpolder and Strijkviertel.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Middelburg, Zeeland, Netherlands

stromenwijk.e: Compound of stromen (“rivers, streams”) + wijk (“neighborhood, town”), after the area's streets which are named after bodies of water.

s.def: a former municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands

sint joosland.e: Named after Sint Joosland, currently part of Nieuw- en Sint Joosland.

s.def: a hamlet in Borsele, Zeeland, Netherlands

scheldeoord.e: Compound of the hydronym Schelde and oord (“place”).

s.def: a hamlet in Borsele, Zeeland, Netherlands

sinoutskerke.e:
First attested as synoteskerke in 1197-1212. Compound of Middle Dutch Synot (a personal name) or Synoud (a personal name) and kerk (“church”).
See also Zealandic Sieskerke.

s.def: a former municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Goes, Zeeland, Netherlands

sluis de piet.e: Compound of sluis (“lock, sluice”) and the hydronym De Piet, likely named after the former owner of lands next to the river, the Chapter of Saint Peter at Utrecht.

s.def: a hamlet in Reimerswaal, Zeeland, Netherlands

schorebrug.e: Attested as Schoorsche brug of Schorebrug in 1899. Compound of the toponym Schore and brug (“bridge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Reimerswaal, Zeeland, Netherlands

stationsbuurt.e:
Attested as Stationsbuurt in 1936. Compound of station (“train station”) and buurt (“hamlet, settlement”).
See also Zealandic De Stationsbuurte.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

sint jansteen.e: First attested as Sancti Ioannis ad Lapidem in 1248. Compound of the name of Saint John the Baptist and Middle Dutch steen (“stone, rock”).

s.def: a former village and former municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands

stoppeldijk.e:
First attested as stopeldic and stoupedich in 1227. Compound of Middle Dutch stoppel (“stalk, twig”) and dijk (“levee”). Compare Spierdijk.
The village was gradually absorbed into Vogelwaarde.

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

schapershoek.e: Attested as Schapers Pr in 1747. Compound of Schaper (a surname) and hoek (“corner, bend”). The settlement was named for its location in the Schapers Polder.

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

scheldevaartshoek.e: Attested as Schelfhoutshoek in 1838-1857. Compound of Schelfhout (a surname) and hoek (“corner, bend”). The first element was gradually reinterpreted into a hydronym.

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

statenboom.e: Attested as Statenboom in 1866. Compound of Staten (“Estates General”) and boom (“barrier”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

stoppeldijkveer.e: Attested as Stoppeldijksche Veer in 1838-1857. Compound of the toponym Stoppeldijk and veer (“ferry, crossing”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

strooienstad.e: Attested as Strooiestad in 1899. Compound of strooien (“made of straw”) and stad (“place”). The settlement was named for the material used to build its first houses. Compare Stroobos, Stroodorp, Stroodorpe and Strooiendorp.

s.def: a city and former municipality of Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

sas van gent.e:
First attested as Tzas in 1573. Derived from sas (“lift-lock”) and the toponym Gent.
See also Zealandic Sas.

s.def: a village in Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

sluiskil.e:
Attested as Sluiskil in 1839. Derived from sluiskil (“drainage channel of a sluice”).
See also Zealandic Sluuskille.

s.def: a hamlet in Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

schapenbout.e:
Attested as Schapenbout in 1840. Compound of the plural form of schaap (“sheep”) and dialectal bout (“remaining section of a collapsed or destroyed levee”). Named for the fact that the inhabitants of the settlement took their sheep to pasture on the remains of the levee.
See also Zealandic Schaop...

s.def: a hamlet in Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

steenovens.e: Attested as De Steenovens in 1838-1857. Derived from the plural form of steenoven (“brick kiln”). Named after a set of former brickworks.

s.def: a hamlet in Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

stroodorpe.e: Attested as Stroodorp in 1838-1857. Compound of stro (“straw”) and dorp (“village, settlement”). The hamlet formed around a set of straw huts built as temporary accommodation for labourers working on the Gent-Terneuzen Channel. Compare Stroobos, Stroodorp, Strooiendorp and Strooienstad.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

serlippenspolder.e: Attested as de Lieven- Noord- Zuid- Serlippens- en Kats-Polder in 1740. Compound of the surname Ser Lippens and polder (“polder”). Named after the creator of the polder, Jan Ser Lippens.

s.def: a former municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands
a city and former municipality of Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

sint anna ter muiden.e:
First attested as in muda in 1273. Derived from the name of Saint Anne and muiden (“river mouth”). The name of Saint Anne was added to distinguish the settlement from Muiden.
See also Zealandic Sint Anna ter Mu.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

schoondijke.e:
First attested as in sconendiic in 1250. Compound of schoon (“clean, free of weeds”) and dijk (“levee”).
See also Zealandic Schwòndieke.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

sint kruis.e:
First attested as jndie prochia van sint crues in 1270. Derived from Sint (“saint”) and kruis (“cross”). Named after a local parish dedicated to the Holy Cross.
See also Zealandic Sinte Kruus.

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

sasput.e: Attested as t Sas in the 18th century. Compound of sas (“lift-lock”) and put (“pool of water, gully”).

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

scherpbier.e:
Attested as Scherpbier fort around 1750. Derived from scherpbier (“low-quality beer”). Named after a former fortress.
See also Zealandic Scherrebier.

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

slapershaven.e: Attested as Slapershaven in 1899. Compound of slaper (“sleeper”) and haven (“harbour, port”).

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

slijkplaat.e:
Attested as De slikplaat in 1838-1857. Compound of slijk (“sludge, mud”) and plaat (“sand bar”).
See also Zealandic Slikplaote.

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

sluissche veer.e: Derived from the toponym Sluis and veer (“ferry, crossing”).

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

smedekensbrugge.e: Attested as SMEETJESBRUG of Smedekensbrug in 1847. Compound of Smedeken (a surname) and brug (“bridge”). Named after a former bridge.

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

steenhoven.e: Attested as Steenhoven in 1843. Compound of steen (“stone”) and the plural form of hof (“farmstead, homestead”).

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

stroopuit.e:
Attested as Stroopuit in 1838-1857. Potentially a compound of stroop (“syrup”) and uit (“without, out of”). May have been the name of a former inn.
See also Zealandic Stroopuut.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almere, Flevoland, Netherlands

stedenwijk.e: Compound of the plural form of stad (“city”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). So named to contrast the neighbourhood with Waterwijk.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Lelystad, Flevoland, Netherlands

schepenwijk.e: Compound of the plural form of schip (“ship, watercraft”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's sub-neighbourhoods are named after various types of watercraft.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almere, Flevoland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Baarn, Utrecht, Netherlands

staatsliedenwijk.e: Both compounds of the plural form of staatsman (“man of state”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhoods' streets are named after various men of state.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almere, Flevoland, Netherlands

seizoenenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of seizoen (“season”) and buurt (“neighbourhood”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after various seasons and months.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almere, Flevoland, Netherlands

stripheldenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of stripheld (“comic book hero”) and buurt (“neighbourhood”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after various comic book characters and artists.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almere, Flevoland, Netherlands

sieradenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of sieraad (“ornament”) and buurt (“neighbourhood”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after various ornaments and pieces of jewellery.

s.def: a hamlet in Noordoostpolder, Flevoland, Netherlands

schokkerhaven.e: Compound of the toponym Schokland and haven (“harbour, port”).

s.def: a hamlet in Zwartewaterland, Overijssel, Netherlands

streukel.e: First attested as strokele in 1400. Compound of Middle Dutch struuc (“tree trunk, pollarded tree”) and lo (“light forest”). Compare Stokkelen.

steenwijkerland.e: Compound of the toponym Steenwijk and land (“land”).

s.def: a village in Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

scheerwolde.e: First attested as scaderwoude around 1313. Compound of Middle Dutch schaar (“share of common land”) and woud (“swamp forest, marsh forest”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

steenwijkerwold.e: First attested as steenwickerwolde in 1376. Compound of the toponym Steenwijk and wold (“swamp forest, marsh forest”).

s.def: a village in Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

sint jansklooster.e: First attested as campus S. Joannis in 1409. Compound of the name of Saint John and klooster (“monastery”). Named after a monastery founded by Johannes van Ommen (ca. 1350-1420), one of the followers of Geert Groote.

s.def: a hamlet in Staphorst, Overijssel, Netherlands

slingenberg.e: First attested as Slingenberg in 1975. Potentially a compound of Middle Dutch slinc (“left-, leftwards”), slinge (“gate in a fence”) or slingen (“wriggle, writhe”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”).

s.def: a hamlet in Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

spoolde.e: First attested as spolderberghe in 1308. Derived from a term cognate to Middle Dutch spelle (“piece of wood”), referring here to an area rich in firewood. Compare Hoog Spul, Laag Spul, Speuld and Speldhurst.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

stadshagen.e: Compound of stad (“city”) and the plural form of haag (“hedge”).

s.def: bitter melon, bitter gourd, Momordica charantia and its fruit

First attested as Schoonoord in 1858. Compound of schoon (“clean”) and oord (“place”). The name was chosen by entrepreneur Jasper Klijn in order to lure settlers to the village. Compare Bonrepas, Kockengen, Koekange, Schoonebeek and Wijngaarden.

solwerd.e: First attested as solwert in 1431. Compound of Middle Dutch sol (“pool, muddy pool”) and Old Frisian wert (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”). Formerly an independent village.

s.def: a village in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

saaxumhuizen.e: First attested as xaxamahusum in 1454. Compound of either the toponym Saexum or Old Frisian Xaxama (a surname) and the plural form of huis (“house”). Compare Saaksum, Lutke Saaxum and Sassenheim.

s.def: a village in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

sauwerd.e: First attested as sawert in 1319. Potentially a compound of Old Frisian sâtha (“sod, turf”) or sâth (“source, well”) and werth (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”). Compare Laad en Zaad.

s.def: a village in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

schouwerzijl.e: First attested as aqueductui in schuwum in 1371. Compound of the toponym Schouwen and zijl (“discharge sluice”).

s.def: a village in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

stitswerd.e: First attested as stukisweret around the second half of the 9th century. Compound of Old Frisian *Stukki (a personal name) and uurð (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands
a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands
a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

schilligeham.e: First attested as apud grangiam in hammis que vulgariter skilgaforwerch appellatur in 1323. Compound of Dutch Low Saxon schillig (“bent”) and ham (“alluvial land in the bend of a river”). The second element was initially voorwerk (“monastic grange”).

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

sint annerhuisjes.e: First attested as Annerhuisjes (Sint-) in 1936. Compound of the toponym Sint Annen and the plural diminutive form of huis (“house”).

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

startenhuizen.e: First attested as stertingahusum in 1391. Potentially derived from Old Frisian stert (“tail”) or dialectal starting (“terrain where goods can temporarily be stored”) and the plural form of huis (“house”). Compare Starting.

s.def: a village in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

saaksum.e: First attested as toe Zaeckzum up den Hollem and to Saexum in 1564. Derived from Old Frisian Sahso (a personal name) and heem (“home, settlement”). Compare Lutke Saaxum, Saaxumhuizen and Sassenheim.

s.def: a village in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

sebaldeburen.e: First attested as in sibaldebuere hove in the 13th century. Compound of Old Frisian Sibald (a personal name) and the plural form of buur (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

schifpot.e: Derived from Dutch Low Saxon schifpot (“kettle in which unusable parts of flax are burned”). The hamlet is named after a kettle used to heat a former inn.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

steentil.e: Compound of steen (“stone”) and til (“fixed bridge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands
a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

suttum.e: First attested as Suttema huys around 1600. Derived either from West Frisian Suttema (a surname) or, if the toponym is older than the surname, from a compound of Old Frisian sūth (“south”) and heem (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands
a former municipality of Friesland, Netherlands

schoterland.e: Calque of West Frisian Skoatterlân, a compound of the toponym Aldskoat and lân (“land”).

s.def: a former municipality of Friesland, Netherlands

skarsterlân.e: Borrowed from West Frisian Skarsterlân, a compound of skar (“communal pasture”) and lân (“land”).

s.def: a village in Achtkarspelen, Friesland, Netherlands

stroobos.e: First attested as Strobus around 1660. Derived from strobos (“bale of straw”). Apparently named after a stack of straw used by canal labourers to shelter from rain. Alternatively, the toponym may refer to an old inn. Compare Stroodorp, Stroodorpe, Strooiendorp and Strooienstad.

s.def: a village in Achtkarspelen, Friesland, Netherlands

surhuisterveen.e: First attested as Surhustera feen in 1603. Calque of West Frisian Surhústerfean, a compound of the toponym Surhuzum and fean (“peat bog, marsh”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Achtkarspelen, Friesland, Netherlands

surhuizum.e: First attested as sutherhusum in 1224. Borrowed from West Frisian Surhuzum, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian sūthar (“southern, south-”) and the dative plural form of hūs (“house”).

s.def: a hamlet in Achtkarspelen, Friesland, Netherlands

surhuizumer mieden.e: Calque of West Frisian Surhuzumer Mieden, derived in turn from the toponym Surhuzum and the plural form of miede (“pasture”).

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

scharnehuizen.e: First attested in 1479. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian Scher (a personal name) or Schern and the plural form of hūs (“house”).

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

sibrandahuis.e: First attested as Sijbranda Huyijs in 1511. Calque of West Frisian Sibrandahûs, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Sibrand (a personal name) and hūs (“house”).

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

steenharst.e: First attested in 1479. Calque of West Frisian Stienharst, a compound of Old Frisian stēn (“stone”) and harst (“overgrown elevated place”). Originally the name of a fortified house.

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

steenvak.e: First attested as int Steenback in 1543. Calque of West Frisian Stienfek, derived in turn from stienfek (“area of a levee filled up with stones around which ground has been excavated”).

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

stiem.e: First attested as Steem in 1511. Borrowed from West Frisian Stiem, derived in turn from the dative plural form of Old Frisian sted (“place, city”). Compare Stedum.

s.def: a village in Dantumadiel, Friesland, Netherlands

sijbrandahuis.e: First attested as Sybrandahuys in 1511. Calque of West Frisian Sibrandahûs, a compound of Sibrand (a personal name) and hûs (“house”).

s.def: a village in Tytsjerksteradiel, Friesland, Netherlands

suameer.e: First attested as suwamer and van suudmerra in 1453. Borrowed from West Frisian Sumar, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian sūth (“south-, southern”) and mar (“lake, pool”).

s.def: a village in Tytsjerksteradiel, Friesland, Netherlands

suawoude.e: First attested as suwald in 1481. Borrowed from West Frisian Suwâld, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian sūth (“south-, southern”) and wald (“marsh forest, swamp forest”).

s.def: a village in Opsterland, Friesland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Tytsjerksteradiel, Friesland, Netherlands

siegerswoude.e:
* (Opsterland) First attested as sigerwolde in 1315. Calque of West Frisian Sigerswâld, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Sieger (a personal name) and wald (“marsh forest, swamp forest”).
* (Tytsjerksteradiel) First attested as sigherswold in 1484. Calque of West Frisian Sigerswâld, ...

s.def: a hamlet in Tytsjerksteradiel, Friesland, Netherlands

suameerderheide.e: Calque of West Frisian Sumarreheide, a compound of the toponym Sumar and heide (“heath, heathland”).

s.def: a hamlet in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

schillaard.e: First attested as scallaert in 1445. Borrowed from West Frisian Skillaerd, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian schil (“seashell”) and werd (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

schrins.e: First attested as scrinze in 1446. Borrowed from West Frisian Skrins, of unknown etymology. Possibly derived from Old Frisian skerd (“notch”) or *Skarde (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi-. Compare Scarcliff.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

stiens.e: First attested as steninge in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Stiens, derived in turn from Old Frisian Stêna (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi-. Compare Staining and Steyning.

s.def: a village in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

snakkerburen.e: First attested as Snackerbuyren in 1664. Borrowed from West Frisian Snakkerbuorren, a compound of an unclear first element and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”). The first element may be cognate with Middle Dutch snacker (“yapping dog”) or Dutch Low Saxon snakken (“speak senselessly”).

s.def: a village in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

swichum.e: First attested as swygghem in the 14th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Swichum, derived in turn from a compound of an unclear first element and Old Frisian hēm (“home, settlement”). The first element may be a personal name.

s.def: a hamlet in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

schilkampen.e: First attested as Schilcampen in 1562. Calque of West Frisian Skilkampen, a compound of skyl (“seashell”) and the plural form of kamp (“enclosed piece of land”). The area around the hamlet was formerly used as a storage site for seashells used in the production of chalk.

s.def: a village in Smallingerland, Friesland, Netherlands

smalle ee.e: First attested as smalena in 1230. Calque of West Frisian Smelle Ie, derived in turn from a hydronym derived from Old Frisian smel (“narrow”) and ē (“watercourse”).

s.def: a hamlet in Smallingerland, Friesland, Netherlands

siteburen.e: First attested as Sytebuyren in 1664. Calque of West Frisian Sitebuorren, a compound of Site (a personal name) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a hamlet in Opsterland, Friesland, Netherlands

selmien.e: First attested as Selmien in 1718. Borrowed from West Frisian Selmien, potentially derived from a compound of syl (“discharge sluice”) and the plural form of miede (“meadow”).

s.def: a hamlet in Opsterland, Friesland, Netherlands

sparjebird.e: First attested as Sparrje bird in 1718. Borrowed from West Frisian Sparjeburd, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian *Sparringa (a surname) and burd (“bank, edge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Opsterland, Friesland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands

sorremorre.e: First attested as sudmorra in 1449. Borrowed from West Frisian Soarremoarre, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian sūth (“south-, southern”) and the hydronym Morra (now called Botmar), derived from mora (“bog, marshland”).

s.def: a hamlet in Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands

schurega.e: First attested as scueringa in 1408. Borrowed from West Frisian Skuorregea, potentially a compound of an otherwise unattested personal name or Old Frisian skūre (“barn, shed”) and gâ (“village, village area”).

s.def: a hamlet in Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands

spitsendijk.e: First attested as Spitsenbrug in 1853. Calque of West Frisian Spitsendyk, a compound of spits (“tapering, protruding”) and dyk (“levee”).

s.def: a hamlet in Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands

sythuizen.e: First attested as zithahwsen in 1465. Calque of West Frisian Sythuzen, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Sijte (a personal name) and the plural form of hūs (“house”).

s.def: a hamlet in Ooststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

schottelenburg.e: Originally the name of a farmhouse.

s.def: a hamlet in Ooststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

schrappinga.e: First attested as Schrappinga in 1622. Derived from West Frisian Schrappinga (a surname).

s.def: a village in Weststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

slijkenburg.e: First attested as slickenborg in 1398. Compound of slijk (“mud, sludge”) and burg (“manor”). Originally the name of a building.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Weststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

sonnega.e: First attested as sonnegae in 1399. Borrowed from West Frisian Sonnegae, derived from a compound of Old Frisian Sonne (a personal name) and gâ (“village, village area”).

s.def: a village in Weststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

spanga.e: First attested as spangghe in 1320. Etymology unknown. Potentially derived from a term cognate to Old English spann (“strip of land”), from Middle Dutch spange (“elongated strip of land”) or from an ancient hydronym. The element gea (“village, village area”) is a later addition that developed und...

s.def: a village in Weststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

steggerda.e: First attested as steggerden and steggarden in 1408. Etymology unknown. Unlikely to be a compound of Old Frisian steg (“narrow road, footpath”) and garda (“garden”). The -da ending developed under the influence of nearby toponyms.

s.def: a hamlet in Weststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

schoterzijl.e: First attested as Scoterzyl in 1543. The toponym references a zijl (“discharge sluice”) near Oudeschoot and Nieuweschoot.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands

skoaterwâld.e: Borrowed from West Frisian Skoaterwâld. Blend of Skoatterlân + wâld (“forest”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Den Helder, North Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Utrecht, Utrecht, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Veenendaal, Utrecht, Netherlands

schepenbuurt.e: All compounds of the plural form of schip (“ship”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhoods' streets are named after ships.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands
a village in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

scharsterbrug.e: First attested as Scharsterbrug in 1847. Compound of the hydronym Scharster and brug (“bridge”).

s.def: a village in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

sint nicolaasga.e: First attested as s'tum Nycolaum in the 13th century. Calque of West Frisian Sint Nikelsgea, a compound of the name of Saint Nicholas and Old Frisian gâ (“village, village area”).

s.def: a village in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

sintjohannesga.e: First attested as Sint Jansga in 1408. Calque of West Frisian Sint-Jânsgea, a compound of the name of Saint John and Old Frisian gâ (“village, village area”).

s.def: a village in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

snikzwaag.e: First attested as sneckswach in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Sniksweach, a compound of Old Frisian snik (“protruding”) and swâg (“pasture, land where cattle is kept”).

s.def: a village in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

sondel.e: First attested as syndele in 1422. Borrowed from West Frisian Sondel, potentially derived from a compound of a variant of Old Frisian sond (“sand”) and lo (“light forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

spannenburg.e: First attested as Spannenberg in 1874. Allegedly named after the owner of the inn around which the settlement developed.

s.def: a city and former municipality of Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

sneek.e:
First attested as sneke in the 13th century. Derived from Old Frisian snik (“protruding, tapering”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an older version of the Frisian name.
See also West Frisian Snits.

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

sijbrandaburen.e: First attested as zibrandaburghe in 1333. Calque of West Frisian Sibrandabuorren, a compound of Old Frisian Sibrand (a personal name) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

sandfirden.e: First attested with certainty as sandforde in 1245. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian sant (“sand”) and forde (“ford, crossing”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name.

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

scharl.e: First attested as scarle in 1412. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian schar (“share in communal land”), schaar (“land next to water, bank”) or skerne (“manure”) and lo (“light forest”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier variant of the Frisian name.

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

scharnegoutum.e: Potentially attested as scharnum in 1200, attested with certainty as scharnum in 1347. Potentially derived from a compound of skarne (“manure, dungheap”) and Old Frisian hēm (“home, settlement”) and a compound of Golda (a personal name) or gold (“gold”) and hēm (“home, settlement”). The Dutch for...

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

schettens.e: First attested as sceddanuurthi in 855. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian *Sceddi (a personal name) or *Scetti suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and wurthi (“artificial mound used for habitation”). The element wurthi disappeared over time. The Dutch form of the toponym was borro...

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

schraard.e: First attested as scadawerth in the 13th century. Potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian skred (“isolated plot of land”) and werd (“artificial mound used for habitation”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name.

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

smallebrugge.e: First attested as smalerbrugghen in 1422. Calque of West Frisian Smelbrêge, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian smel (“narrow”) and bregge (“bridge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

swaanwerd.e: First attested as Swammert in 1543. Borrowed from West Frisian Swaenwert, potentially derived from Old Frisian Swaan (a personal name) and wert (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

sandfirderrijp.e: First attested as Tho Sanfoard in die Cleijne Rijp in 1511. Calque of West Frisian Sânfurder Ryp, a compound of the toponym Sânfurd and ryp (“edge, bank”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

scharneburen.e: First attested as Olpherder buyren in 1664. Borrowed from West Frisian Skearnebuorren, a compound of skerne (“manure, dungheap”) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

sieswerd.e: First attested as syswert in 1403. Borrowed from West Frisian Syswert.

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

sjungadijk.e: First attested as sioungha dyck in 1478. Calque of West Frisian Sjongedyk, a compound of Old Frisian Sjunga (a surname) and dīk (“levee”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

sotterum.e: Potentially attested as sotrenheim in 944, attested with certainty as Sottum in 1543. Borrowed from West Frisian Sotterum, derived from a compound of Old Frisian *Sôhtra (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a village in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

spannum.e: First attested as spannum in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Spannum, of uncertain etymology. Potentially derived from the dative plural form of a term cognate to Old English spann (“span of a hand, strip of land”). Compare Spanbroek, Spang, Spanga and Spangen.

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

schelum.e: First attested as schyldum in 1453. Etymology unknown. Potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian Schelte (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier form of the Frisian name.

s.def: a village in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

schingen.e: First attested as schengen around 1400. Potentially derived from a prehistoric hydronym. The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name.

s.def: a village in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

slappeterp.e: First attested as clepesdor [read: sleperdorp] in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Slappeterp, derived in turn from a compound of an unclear first element and Old Frisian dorp (“village, artificial mound used for habitation”). The first element may be a term cognate to Old English sli...

s.def: a village and former municipality of Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

sint annaparochie.e: First attested as altoenae, s. annen parochie in 1570. Compound of the name of Saint Anne and parochie (“parish”). Originally named Altena, after the former domicile of one of the investors in the development of the Bildt. Compare Sint Anna and Sint-Annaland.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

sint jacobiparochie.e: First attested as wijngaerden, s. iacobus parochie in 1570. Compound of the name of Saint Jacob and parochie (“parish”).

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

stad niks.e: First attested as Stad-Niks in 1850. Derived from stad (“city”) and niks (“nothing, zilch”). The name pokes fun at the small size of the hamlet. Originally the name of a house.

s.def: a village in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

schalsum.e: First attested as scalkessum in 1319. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian scalk (“servant, lackey”) or Scalk (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

sexbierum.e: First attested as sixtisberen in the 13th century. Derived from a compound of the name of Saint Sixtus and the dative plural form of bêre (“barn, house”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name. Compare Bierum and Burum.

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

salverd.e: First attested as ztalwert in 1406. Borrowed from West Frisian Salvert, derived from a compound of Old Frisian Tsjalle (a personal name) or thiâl (“wheel”) and werd (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

sopsum.e: First attested as sopsem in 1433. Borrowed from West Frisian Sopsum, derived from a compound of Old Frisian Soppe (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Goeree-Overflakkee, South Holland, Netherlands

sommelsdijk.e:
First attested as sommeldijch in 1324. Likely a compound of the hydronym Sonnemaer (potentially derived from a compound of a term cognate to zwin and Old Dutch meri (“lake”)) and Middle Dutch dijk (“levee”). Compare Son and Zonnemaire.
See also Zealandic Sommerdiek.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Goeree-Overflakkee, South Holland, Netherlands

stad aan 't haringvliet.e:
First attested as uitgors, ... geheeten die stat in 1395. Derived from Middle Dutch stat (“embankment, landing place”) and the hydronym Haringvliet. The first element was gradually reinterpreted to stad (“city”).
See also Zealandic Stad an 't Haeriengvliet.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Goeree-Overflakkee, South Holland, Netherlands

stellendam.e: Attested as t Stelle Gors around 1750. Compound of stelle (“artificial mound constructed on land located outside a levee for emergency shelter by shepherds and cattle”) and dam (“dam”). Named after the dam linking Goedereede to Overflakkee.

s.def: a hamlet in Voorne aan Zee, South Holland, Netherlands

strype.e: Attested as De Strijp in 1565. Derived from strijp (“strip of land, elongated, narrow plot of land”).

s.def: a hamlet in Voorne aan Zee, South Holland, Netherlands

stuifakker.e: First attested as de polder van tStuyffacker in 1565. Compound of stuif (“drift-sand”) and akker (“farmland”).

s.def: a village in Nissewaard, South Holland, Netherlands

simonshaven.e: First attested as her simonshaven in 1431. Compound of Middle Dutch Simon (a personal name) and haven (“harbour”). Named after Simon van Markenburg (d. 1331), a nobleman who developed land around the settlement.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Nissewaard, South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Nissewaard, South Holland, Netherlands

sterrenkwartier.e: Compound of the plural form of ster (“star”) and kwartier (“quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after celestial objects.

s.def: a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

schuddebeurs en simonshaven.e: Named after the Schuddebeurs estate and Simonshaven.

s.def: a hamlet in Barendrecht, South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Barendrecht, South Holland, Netherlands

smitshoek.e:
Attested as Smitshoek in 1839-1859. Compound of Smits (a surname) and hoek (“corner”). Possibly linked to Smeetsland.
The neighbourhood is named after the hamlet.

s.def: a village in Ridderkerk, South Holland, Netherlands

slikkerveer.e: First attested as slykerveerstal and slyckerveerstal in 1423 and 1424. Compound of the toponym *Slijk (derived from Middle Dutch slyc (“mud, sludge”)) and veerstal (“ferry”), later reduced to veer (“ferry”).

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of Ridderkerk, South Holland, Netherlands

strevelshoek.e: First attested as strevelshoeck in 1340. Compound of an unclear first element and Middle Dutch hoeck (“corner, isolated place”). The first element may be a personal name or a term reinterpreted to Middle Dutch strevel (“bush, shrub”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hendrik-Ido-Ambacht, South Holland, Netherlands

sophiapolder.e: Compound of Sophia (a personal name) and polder (“polder”). Named after Sophie of Württemberg.

s.def: a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Zwijndrecht, South Holland, Netherlands

sterrenbeeldenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of sterrenbeeld (“constellation”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after constellations.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Hoeksche Waard, South Holland, Netherlands

strijensas.e: Attested as t Sas van Stryen in 1617. Compound of the toponym Strijen and sas (“lift-lock”).

* (Hoeksche Waard, near Zuid-Beijerland) Derived from schenkeldijk (“levee connecting two other levees”).

sint anthoniepolder.e: First attested as Poldris s. Anthonii in 1502. Compound of the name of Saint Anthony and polder (“polder”).

sliedrecht.e: First attested as overslydrecht in 1203. Potentially a compound of the hydronym *Slie (perhaps cognate to slijk (“sludge, mud”) or slijm (“slime”)) and -drecht (“ferry, watercourse”).

schelluinen.e: First attested as scalun in 1220. Named after Ascalon. Compare Schaluinen.

stolwijk.e: First attested as stolwyc and stulwyc in 1330. Presumably a compound of Middle Dutch stolle (“lump, chunk”) and wyc (“farmstead, village, settlement”).

schoonouwen.e: First attested as sconouwe in 1248. Compound of Middle Dutch scone (“clean, free of weeds”) and the plural form of ouw (“land next to a watercourse”). Compare Schonauwen.

schuwacht.e: Attested as Schuagt in 1783. Etymology unknown. Potentially derived from a compound of Middle Dutch schu (“scarecrow”) and wacht (“watch”).

stormpolder.e: First attested as stormspolre in 1364. Compound of Middle Dutch Storm (a personal name) and polre (“polder”). Possibly named after the sponsor of the polder's construction.

sterrenwijk.e:
* (Utrecht) Compound of the plural form of ster (“star, celestial body”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after planets.

seghwaert.e: First attested as segwart in 1280-1287. Compound of Middle Dutch seg (“sedge, Carex sp.”) and werde (“land next to water, riverine island”). The current spelling of the toponym is deliberately archaic.

a village and former municipality of Midden-Delfland, South Holland, Netherlands

schipluiden.e: First attested as scipliede in the 12th century. Derived from a hydronym, derived in turn from Middle Dutch schiplede (“navigable watercourse”). The second element was gradually reinterpreted to lieden (“people”).

sluispolder.e: Compound of sluis (“sluice, lock”) and polder (“polder”). Named after a polder.

scheepvaartkwartier.e: Compound of scheepvaart (“shipping”) and kwartier (“quarter, neighbourhood”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

stadsdriehoek.e: Compound of stad (“city”) and driehoek (“triangle”). The neighbourhood is named for its triangular shape and central location within Rotterdam, roughly matching the oldest core of the city.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

schiemond.e: Compound of the hydronym Schie and mond (“mouth”). Originally the name of a harbour.

s.def: a neighbourhood and former municipality of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

schiebroek.e: First attested as scieboec in 1400. Compound of the hydronym Schie and Middle Dutch bruec (“marsh, marshland”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

sportdorp.e: Compound of sport (“sports”) and dorp (“village, settlement”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after various sports.

smeetsland.e: Attested as Dirck Smeets Landeken in 1514. Compound of Smeets (a surname) and land (“land”). Originally the name of a polder. Possibly linked to Smitshoek.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

struisenburg.e: First attested as de Struisvogel. Compound of struis (“ostrich, Struthio sp.”) and burg (“manor, mansion”). Originally the name of a mansion, which was named in turn after a nearby windmill.

a neighbourhood of The Hague, South Holland, Netherlands

scheveningen.e: First attested as scevenighe and scevenighen in 1280–1287. Potentially derived from the dative plural form of Old Dutch *Sceven (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing-.

s.def: a neighbourhood of The Hague, South Holland, Netherlands

spoorwijk.e: Compound of spoor (“railway”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood was named for its proximity to a major railway station.

s.def: a neighbourhood of The Hague, South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Voorschoten, South Holland, Netherlands

starrenburg.e: Attested as Starrenburgerpolder in 1809. Named after a polder and former house.

a neighbourhood of Leiden, South Holland, Netherlands

stevenshof.e: Attested as Stevenhofjespolder in 1674. Compound of the name of Saint Stephen and hof (“court”). Originally the name of a polder owned by the Convent of Saint Stephen (a poorhouse) in Leiden.

examples: Wel gaan gesprekspartners veelal onbewust uit van dit samenwerkingsbeginsel, bijvoorbeeld dat ze op een vraag een antwoord verwachten.
Volgens de taalkundige Paul Grice geldt er in de communicatie een samenwerkingsbeginsel.

samenwerkingsbeginsel.e: From samenwerking (“cooperation”) + -s- + beginsel (“principle”), calque of English cooperative principle.

a neighbourhood of Lisse, South Holland, Netherlands

schilderswijk.e: Both compounds of the plural form of schilder (“painter”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhoods' streets are named after famous painters.

spoelwijk.e: First attested as spoelwyck in 1360 and 1361. Potentially a compound of Middle Dutch spoel (“rinsing water”) and wyc (“farmstead, village, settlement”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Alphen aan den Rijn, South Holland, Netherlands

stromenbuurt.e: Compound of stromen (“rivers, streams”) + buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”), after its streets which are named for various watercourses.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Alphen aan den Rijn, South Holland, Netherlands

steinenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of -stein (“castle”), a toponymic suffix, and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The names of the neighbourhood's streets end in the element -stein.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Alphen aan den Rijn, South Holland, Netherlands

snijdelwijk.e: First attested as snodelwiic and snedelwike in 1280-1287. Compound of Middle Dutch snodel (“poor sod, dummy”) and wyc (“farmstead, village, settlement”). Compare Knollemanshoek, Snel in Snel en Polanen and Snelleveld.

s.def: a hamlet in Gouda, South Holland, Netherlands

stolwijkersluis.e: First attested as Stolwijker sluis in 1839-1859. Compound of the toponym Stolwijk and sluis (“sluice, lock”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Gouda, South Holland, Netherlands

sportbuurt.e: Compound of sport (“sports”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after various sporting events.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Gouda, South Holland, Netherlands

slagenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of slag (“division of a polder”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The names of the neighbourhood's streets all end in slag.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Bodegraven-Reeuwijk, South Holland, Netherlands

sluipwijk.e: First attested as slupewiic in 1280-1287. Compound of an unclear first element and Middle Dutch wyc (“farmstead, village, settlement”) or wyc (“inlet, side channel”). The first element may be related to sluipen (“sneak, slink”) referring here to the hidden location of the settlement.

s.def: a hamlet in Nieuwkoop, South Holland, Netherlands

slikkendam.e: Attested as Slickendam in 1575. Compound of the plural form of Middle Dutch slyck (“peat dredgings”) and dam (“dam”).

sint nicolaas.e: Named after Saint Nicholas. According to a local etymology, the settlement was once owned by one Nicolaas van der Biest (1808-1873), whose subordinates were forced to address him as shon (“master”) Nicolaas. The toponym was then reinterpreted to the name of Saint Nicholas.

sabana grandi.e: Borrowed from Papiamentu Sabana Grandi, derived from sabana (“savannah”) and grandi (“big, great”).

sabana liber.e: Borrowed from Papiamentu Sabana Liber, derived from sabana (“savannah”) and liber (“free”).

sint willibrordus.e: Named after Saint Willibrord, the patron of the village church.

scharloo.e: Potentially derived from a compound of schaar (“share, sector”) and lo (“light forest”). According to a local etymology, the neighbourhood was named after a French lady.

saliña.e: Borrowed from Papiamentu Saliña, derived from saliña (“salt lake, inland lake”).

seru fortuna.e: Borrowed from Papiamentu Seru Fortuna, derived from seru (“mountain”) and the toponym Fortuna.

souax.e: Borrowed from Papiamentu Souax. Named after a former plantation.

stenen koraal.e: Derived from stenen (“stone-”) and a variant of kraal (“corral”).

schelpwijk.e: Compound of schelp (“shell”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The first element is a jocular reference to the builders of the neighbourhood, the Royal Dutch Shell oil company. Compare Essoville, Lagoville and Standardville.

sint jacob.e: Named after Saint Jacob. Originally the name of an estate.

sabadeco.e: Derived from a contraction of Santa Barbara Development Corporation, the name of the company which built the neighbourhood.

s.def: a hamlet in Diemen, North Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Diemen, North Holland, Netherlands

scheepskwartier.e: Compound of schip (“ship”) and kwartier (“quarter, neighbourhood”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after ship types.

spiegelzicht.e: Compound of the toponym Het Spiegel and zicht (“view”).

schrijverskwartier.e: Compound of the plural form of schrijver (“writer”) and kwartier (“quarter, neighbourhood”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after famous writers.

schilderskwartier.e: Compound of the plural form of schilder (“painter”) and kwartier (“quarter, neighbourhood”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after famous painters.

schellingwoude.e: First attested as scellingwoude in 1275. Compound of Middle Dutch schellinge (“boundary, border”) and wout (“marsh forest, swamp forest”). Compare Schellinkhout and Terschelling.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

spaarndammerbuurt.e: Compound of the toponym Spaarndam and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). Named after a street.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

slotermeer.e: Attested as Slooter Meer in 1615. Compound of the toponym Sloten and meer (“lake”). Named after a former lake.

slotervaart.e: Attested as De Slooter vaert in 1687. Compound of the toponym Sloten and vaart (“navigable water”). Named after a canal.

sloterdijk.e: First attested as sloterdam in 1395-1396. Compound of the toponym Sloten and Middle Dutch dyk (“levee”). The second element was originally dam (“dam”). Formerly an independent village.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

schinkelbuurt.e: Compound of the hydronym Schinkel and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”).

stadionbuurt.e: Compound of stadion (“stadium”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). Named after a former stadium.

sporenburg.e: Compound of spoor (“railway”) and burg (“manor, castle”). The neighbourhood was formerly used as a marshalling yard.

schardam.e: First attested as scaderdam in 1319. Etymology uncertain. Potentially derived from a hypercorrect rendering of Middle Dutch schaerdam (“dam constructed on the bank of a body of water”). Compare Scharwoude.

singelwijk.e: Compound of singel (“canal”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”).

stompetoren.e: Attested as Kerck in 1680. Compound of stomp (“blunt, obtuse”) and toren (“tower”). The settlement was named after the shape of the tower of the village church.

a neighbourhood of Alkmaar, North Holland, Netherlands

stadhouderskwartier.e: Compound of the plural form of stadhouder (“stadtholder”) and kwartier (“quarter, neighbourhood”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after stadtholders of the Dutch Republic.

schermereiland.e: Compound of the hydronym Schermer and eiland (“island”).

saendelft.e: Compound of the hydronym Zaan and delft (“dug or canalised watercourse”). The name was modelled after Assendelft.

spaarndam-oost.e: First attested as sparnam in 1253. Compound of the hydronym Spaarne and Middle Dutch dam (“dam”). The element oost (“east”) was added to distinguish the settlement from Spaarndam-West.

spaarnwoude.e: First attested as spirnerewalt in the first half of the 11th century. Compound of the hydronym Spaarne (of unknown etymology) and Middle Dutch wout (“marsh forest, swamp forest”).

spaarndam.e: Named after Spaarndam, currently divided into Spaarndam-West and Spaarndam-Oost.

spaarndam-west.e: First attested as sparnam in 1253. Compound of the hydronym Spaarne and Middle Dutch dam (“dam”). The element west (“west”) was added to distinguish the settlement from Spaarndam-Oost.

sportsliedenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of sportsman (“sportsman”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after famous sportsmen.

stedenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of stad (“city”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after cities.

slachthuiswijk.e: Compound of slachthuis (“abattoir”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). Named after a former abattoir.

a neighbourhood of Haarlem, North Holland, Netherlands

schalkwijk.e:
* (Utrecht) First attested as scalwiich, scalcwiich and scalewiich in 1130-1161. Compound of Old Dutch scalc (“servant”) and wīk (“farmstead, village, settlement”).
* (Noord-Holland) Compound of Old Dutch scalc (“servant”) or a personal name derived from scalc and wīk (“farmstead, village, settle...

s.def: a neighbourhood of Haarlem, North Holland, Netherlands

sinnevelt.e: Attested as Sinnevelt in 1680. Potentially a compound of an unknown fytonym and veld (“field”). Named after a former estate.

santpoort-noord.e: First attested as santpoort in 1389. Etymology unknown. Not derived from Medieval Latin sanctus (“holy”) and porta (“gate”). The toponym is also not a compound of Middle Dutch sant (“sand”) and port (“harbour”), as the area around Velsen was not used for sand extraction until the Early Modern era...

s.def: a village in Velsen, North Holland, Netherlands

santpoort-zuid.e: First attested as santpoort in 1389. Etymology unknown. Not derived from Medieval Latin sanctus (“holy”) and porta (“gate”). The toponym is also not a compound of Middle Dutch sant (“sand”) and port (“harbour”), as the area around Velsen was not used for sand extraction until the Early Modern era...

s.def: a neighbourhood of Beverwijk, North Holland, Netherlands

sint aagtendorp.e: First attested as agathenkiricha and agathenkyricha in the first half of the 11th century. Compound of the name of Saint Agatha and Middle Dutch dorp (“village, settlement”). The toponym was once an alternative name of Beverwijk.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Heemskerk, North Holland, Netherlands

slotherenbuurt.e: Compound of slotheer (“lord of a castle”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after noblemen.

schulpstet.e: Attested as Schulpstet in 1839-1859. Compound of a variant of schelp (“shell”) and stede (“place”). The toponym may refer to a place where shell-sand was loaded onto barges headed for Alkmaar.

s.def: a hamlet in Castricum, North Holland, Netherlands

starting.e: Attested as Starting in 1639. Derived from dialectal starting (“terrain where goods can be temporarily stored, storage site for sand, shells, gravel etc.”).

s.def: a hamlet in Castricum, North Holland, Netherlands

stierop.e: Attested as Stierp in 1639. Compound of a term cognate to stuur (“wild, rough, turbulent”) and Old Dutch *apa (“river, watercourse”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Dijk en Waard, North Holland, Netherlands

sint pancras.e: First attested as pancraets in 1433. Named after Saint Pancras of Rome.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Dijk en Waard, North Holland, Netherlands

stad van de zon.e: Derived from stad (“city”), van (“of”), de (“the”) and zon (“sun”). The neighbourhood was conceived as a fully carbon-neutral area.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Koggenland, North Holland, Netherlands

scharwoude.e: First attested as scaderwoude around 1312. Etymology uncertain. Potentially a compound of Middle Dutch scaer (“shore, bank”) and wout (“marsh forest, swamp forest”). Compare Schardam.

s.def: a village in Koggenland, North Holland, Netherlands

spierdijk.e: First attested as spierdijck in 1365. Compound of Middle Dutch spier (“long, tough rhizome of a rush”) and dyc (“levee”). Compare Stoppeldijk.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Drechterland, North Holland, Netherlands

schellinkhout.e: First attested as scellinchout around 1312. Compound of Middle Dutch schellinge (“boundary”) and wout (“marsh forest, swamp forest”). Compare Schellingwoude and Terschelling.

s.def: a municipality of North Holland, Netherlands

stede broec.e: Named after Grootebroek, a former city composed of Bovenkarspel and Grootebroek.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Medemblik, North Holland, Netherlands

sijbekarspel.e: First attested as siboutskerspel around 1312. Compound of Middle Dutch Sybout (a personal name) and kercspel (“parish, village with a church”).

s.def: a village in Hollands Kroon, North Holland, Netherlands

slootdorp.e: Attested as Slootdorp in 1936. Compound of the hydronym Sloot (derived from sloot (“ditch”)) and dorp (“village”). Named after a former gully.

s.def: a hamlet in Hollands Kroon, North Holland, Netherlands

smerp.e: First attested as smyeriep in 1432. Likely a compound of an unclear first element and Middle Dutch ripe (“edge, bank”). The first element may be cognate to dialectal smeu (“weak, soft”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hollands Kroon, North Holland, Netherlands

spoorbuurt.e: First attested as Halte in 1869. Compound of spoor (“railway”) and buurt (“hamlet, small settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Schagen, North Holland, Netherlands

schagerwaard.e: Compound of the toponym Schagen and waard (“polder complex surrounded by water”). Named after a polder.

s.def: a hamlet in Schagen, North Holland, Netherlands

schoorldam.e: Attested as Schoorldam in 1569. Compound of the toponym Schoorl and dam (“dam”).

s.def: a hamlet in Schagen, North Holland, Netherlands

slootgaard.e: Attested as De Slootgert in 1680. Compound of sloot (“ditch”) and gaard (“garden”). Originally the name of a polder.

s.def: a village in Schagen, North Holland, Netherlands

schagerbrug.e: Attested as Schagerbrugge in 1613. Compound of the toponym Schagen and brug (“bridge”).

s.def: a village in Schagen, North Holland, Netherlands

sint maartensbrug.e: Attested as Sinte Maertensbrugge in 1613. Compound of the toponym Sint Maarten and brug (“bridge”).

s.def: a village in Schagen, North Holland, Netherlands

sint maartensvlotbrug.e: Attested as St Maartens Vlotbrug in 1839-1859. Compound of the toponym Sint Maarten and vlotbrug (“retractable floating bridge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Schagen, North Holland, Netherlands

sint maartenszee.e: Compound of the toponym Sint Maarten and zee (“sea”).

s.def: a hamlet in Schagen, North Holland, Netherlands

stolpervlotbrug.e: Attested as Stolper Vlotbrug in 1839-1859. Compound of the toponym De Stolpen and vlotbrug (“retractable floating bridge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Texel, North Holland, Netherlands

spijkdorp.e: First attested as spike in 1436. Compound of Middle Dutch spike (“headland, spit”) and dorp (“village, settlement”).

s.def: a village in Bunschoten, Utrecht, Netherlands

spakenburg.e: First attested as spakenburg in 1435. Likely a compound of Middle Dutch spake (“stake, bar”) and borch (“fortified settlement, castle”). The toponym may refer to a levee reinforced with wooden poles.

s.def: a hamlet in Baarn, Utrecht, Netherlands

soestdijk.e: First attested as zoesdijck in 1394. Compound of the toponym Soest and Middle Dutch dyc (“raised road in a wetland”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Baarn, Utrecht, Netherlands
a village in Soest, Utrecht, Netherlands

soestduinen.e: Attested as Soestduinen in 1936. Compound of the toponym Soest and the plural form of duin (“dune”). The village is located in the middle of a sand dune complex.

s.def: a village in Soest, Utrecht, Netherlands

soesterberg.e: First attested as BERG, Soesterberg of den Berg in 1840. Compound of the toponym Soest and berg (“hill, elevated place”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Soest, Utrecht, Netherlands

soester hoogt.e: Derived from the toponym Soest and hoogt (“elevated place”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Soest, Utrecht, Netherlands

smitsveen.e: Compound of Smits (a surname) and veen (“peat bog, marsh”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Soest, Utrecht, Netherlands

soesterveen.e: Compound of the toponym Soest and veen (“peat bog, marsh”).

s.def: a hamlet in Amersfoort, Utrecht, Netherlands

stoutenburg-noord.e: Compound of the toponym Stoutenburg and noord (“north”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amersfoort, Utrecht, Netherlands

soesterkwartier.e: Compound of the toponym Soest and kwartier (“quarter, neighbourhood”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amersfoort, Utrecht, Netherlands

schothorst.e: Attested as Schothorst in 1847. Compound of schot (“enclosed space, fenced-in land”) and horst (“overgrown elevated place”). Named after a nearby estate.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Leusden, Utrecht, Netherlands

stoutenburg.e:
First attested as stoltenberch in 1262. Compound of Middle Dutch stout (“powerful, strong, undaunted”) and either berch (“hill, elevated place”) or borch (“fortified settlement, castle”). Named after a nearby estate and former castle.
Compare Hardenberg, Montfoort, Montfort and Sterkenburg.

s.def: a hamlet in Leusden, Utrecht, Netherlands

snorrenhoef.e: First attested as Snorrenhoeff in 1547. Compound of an unclear first element and hoeve (“farmstead, homestead”). The first element is unlikely to be a personal name.

examples: Nergens ter wereld waarschijnlijk wordt een auto, hoe oud en versleten ook, zo gewaardeerd en begeerd als in het land der Skipetaren. Hier voldoet zelfs een Trabant. Decennia lang was gemotoriseerd vervoer voorbehouden aan gepriviligeerde partijbonzen.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Veenendaal, Utrecht, Netherlands

schrijverswijk.e: Compound of the plural form of schrijver (“author”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after famous authors.

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of Utrechtse Heuvelrug, Utrecht, Netherlands

sterkenburg.e: First attested as starkenbergh in 1307. Compound of Middle Dutch starc (“strong”) and borch (“fortified settlement, castle”). Named after a former castle. Compare Hardenberg, Montfoort, Montfort and Stoutenburg.

s.def: a hamlet in Wijk bij Duurstede, Utrecht, Netherlands

steenen brug.e: Derived from stenen (“stone-, made of stone”) and brug (“bridge”). Named after an inn, named in turn after a nearby bridge.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Houten, Utrecht, Netherlands

schonenburg.e: Compound of Middle Dutch schone (“clean, free of weeds”) and borch (“fortified settlement, castle”). Named after a medieval fortified farmstead.

schonauwen.e:
First attested as sconouwe in 1248. Compound of Middle Dutch schone (“clean, free of weeds”) and ouwe (“land next to water, riverine island”).
Compare Schoonouwen.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Vijfheerenlanden, Utrecht, Netherlands

schoonrewoerd.e: First attested as scoenrewrth in 1166. Compound of Old Dutch skōni (“clean, free of weeds, pretty”) and wrth (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of Oudewater, Utrecht, Netherlands

snelrewaard.e: First attested as snoyelrewaert around 1307. Compound of the toponym Snel (for which see Snel en Polanen) and Middle Dutch werde (“polder complex surrounded by water”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Woerden, Utrecht, Netherlands

snel en polanen.e: Named after the polders Snel and Polanen. Snel is first attested as Snel in 1615. Polanen is first attested as polanen in 1281-1284. Snel is derived from a contracted compound of Middle Dutch snode (“poor, bad”) and lo (“light forest”). Polanen is derived from Van Polanen (a noble surname). The p...

stokkelaarsbrug.e: First attested as Stoppelaers brug in 1575. Compound of a first element which may be a nickname or a personal name and brug (“bridge”). Named after a nearby bridge. Compare Hakkelaarsbrug.

s.def: a hamlet in Stichtse Vecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

scheendijk.e: First attested as De Scheyding in 1746. Compound of dialectal scheen (“boundary, border”) and dijk (“levee”).

spengen.e:
First attested as spanien in 1217. Derived from Middle Dutch Spaengen (“Spain”). Named after the country in Europe.
Compare Demmerik, Hoograven, Laagraven, Kamerik, Kockengen, Kortrijk, Parijsch, Pavijen, Portengen and Snel en Polanen.

s.def: a former municipality of Utrecht, Netherlands

stichts loenen.e: Derived from the term Stichts (“pertaining to or of the Prince-Bishopric of Utrecht”) and the toponym Loenen aan de Vecht.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Utrecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

schaakbuurt.e: Compound of schaak (“chess”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after chess moves and famous chess players.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Utrecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

strijkviertel.e:
First attested as striitvierdel in 1381-1383. Compound of Middle Dutch strijt (“conflict, fight”) and vierdel (“quarter, quarter of a hoeve”). The toponym refers to a piece of land which was the subject of a territorial dispute.
Compare Cabauw, Hebrecht, Kijfhoek, Kijfwaard, Kibbelgaarn, Kibbelve...

s.def: a neighbourhood of Utrecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

stadsdam.e: First attested as Stadsdam in 1661. Compound of stad (“city”) and dam (“dam”). Named after a former dam constructed in 1564 by the city of Utrecht.

stokkem.e: First attested as stockheim in 1181. Compound of Old Dutch stok (“tree trunk, stick”) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

The element Sint-Huibrechts was added to distinguish the settlement from Kaulille by way of a reference to a loc...

substitutie.e: Borrowed from Middle French substitution, from Latin substitutiō.

substitueren.e: Borrowed from French substituer, from Latin substituō.

Zijn we daarom na bijna 48 jaar Srefidensi tot op het bot verarmd; uitgezogen en geplunderd door onze eigen mensen, die we zelf al juichend en zingend daar steeds gezet hebben: hoezee, hupsakee, strey de'f strey!

examples: In verband met 47 jaar staatkundige onafhankelijkheid op 25 november zijn woensdag 127 Surinamers onderscheiden door president Santokhi. Traditioneel worden aan de vooravond van de Srefidensi-viering personen die zich verdienstelijk hebben gemaakt voor land en volk door de president van de republiek gedecoreerd.

srefidensi.e: Unadapted borrowing from Sranan Tongo srefidensi (“independence”).

strontpot.e: From Middle Dutch strontpot, equivalent to stront + pot.

stuud.e: From studie, studeren or a related term.

snateren.e: From Middle Dutch snateren; ultimately an onomatopoeic formation with the suffix -eren. Cognate with German schnattern.

spotlijster.e: Compound of spotten (“to mock”) + lijster (“thrush”). The literal translation of mockingbird is spotvogel, which in Dutch instead refers to a different species of bird native to Europe.

schuimalg.e: Compound of schuim (“foam”) + alg (“alga”), named for the fact that its blooms (in the form of huge wads of foam) are well-known for washing ashore in the Netherlands during spring.

examples: De blauwborst is mijn schaamsoort, ik kan hem maar niet vinden.

scharrelaar.e: From scharrelen (“to mess about, gather, scavenge”) + -aar.

spix' ara.e: Named for (and first documented by) Johann Baptist von Spix, a nineteenth-century German biologist.

skorro.e: Borrowed from Sranan Tongo skoro. Doublet of school and sjoel.

stekelstaart.e: From stekel (“prickle, spine”) + staart (“tail”). Compare English spinetail, which refers to many of the species also indicated with this Dutch term.
