smeerwortel.e: Compound of smeren (“to smear”) + wortel (“root”), as these plants were used in ointments in traditional medicine.

examples: Amsterdam wordt omringd door groene scheggen die tot diep in de stad reiken.

steenbreek.e: From Middle Dutch steenbreke; compare Old High German steinbrehha, Old Saxon stēnbreka. By surface analysis, steen (“stone”) + breken (“to break”), thus literally, “stone-breaker”. The suffix -e (corresponding to the Germanic agent noun suffix *-ô) visible in the Middle Dutch has mostly vanished ...

steenbreekvaren.e: Compound of steenbreek (“saxifrage, lit. stone-breaker”) + varen (“fern”), likely so-named because it generally grows on walls and rocky terrain. Name aside, it is unrelated to the saxifrages sensu stricto.

sleedoorn.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch sleedorn. By surface analysis, slee + doorn. Compare German Schlehdorn, West Flemish sleedôorn.

sint-jacobsvlinder.e: From vlinder, perhaps named after its host plant, jakobskruiskruid.

soelaas.e: From Middle Dutch solas, solaes, soelaes, from Old French solaz, solas, from Latin sōlācium.

sporenkievit.e: From spoor (“spur”) + -en- + kievit (“lapwing”). So named for the small spurs visible on the front of its wings (see right wing of bird pictured).

siberische lis.e: Literally, “Siberian iris”, named for part of its range: it is found from Western Europe (though not considered native in the Netherlands) all the way to Siberia.

schriftrol.e: Equivalent to schrift (“book; scripture”) + rol (“roll, scroll”).

"Wij zijn ons ervan bewust dat dit geen gemakkelijke opgave is, dat de nodige hobbels en sibibusi's nog overwonnen moeten worden, maar voelt u zich gesterkt door het overweldigend vertrouwen dat het Surinaams volk heeft uitgesproken op 25 mei 2020."

sibibusi.e: Unadapted borrowing from Sranan Tongo sibibusi.

suggestie.e: Borrowed from French suggestion, and Latin suggestiō.

Suryoye zijn de Syrisch-orthodoxe christenen van wie velen uit het Midden-Oosten zijn gevlucht tijdens de Armeense genocide vanaf 1915 tijdens het Ottomaanse Rijk. Cognate to English scrimp (Old English sċrimman (“to shrink”)). Related to Dutch schrompelen and schrinken.

strontweer.e: Compound of stront + weer. Compare French temps de merde.

sluipreclame.e: As if from sluipen (“to sneak”) + reclame (“advertising, advertisement”).

sommig.e: From Middle Dutch sommich, summich, zomich, somich, from som (“any, some”) + -ich. Cognate with Middle Low German sōmich, sommich, summich, Old Frisian sumich (whence West Frisian sommige).

stipuleren.e: Borrowed from French stipuler, from Latin stipulārī.

Als met een toverslag begint het geluid van de siksijoeroe, een krekeltje, zoo genoemd, omdat het zich met zonsondergang, dus omstreeks zes uur, laat hooren.

stabilisatie.e: Borrowed from French stabilisation. Equivalent to stabiliseren + -atie.

soortement.e: Reanalysis of sortiment (by-form of assortiment) as soort + -e- + -ment.

sjibbolet.e: From Biblical Hebrew שִׁבֹּלֶת / שיבולת (šibbṓleṯ, “stream, torrent”). More at shibboleth.

schrepel.e: From Middle Dutch schrepel, from schrepen. Equivalent to schrapen + -el.

straatpizza.e: Compound of straat + pizza. A similar visual metaphor is found in English road pizza.

sporkehout.e: The first element derives, with metathesis, from Middle Dutch sproc (“twig, faggot”) (whence sprokkelen (“to gather [brushwood]”), sprokkelhout (“brushwood”)), perhaps in reference to the brittleness of the wood: compare the genus name Frangula, deriving from Latin frangulus (“fragile”), and also...

spaghetticode.e: From spaghetti + code, calque of English spaghetti code.

schram.e: From Middle Dutch scramme. Further etymology unknown. Compare German Schramme.

spiering.e: From Middle Dutch spierinc, from Old Dutch spierinc, from *spīr (“muscle, summit”) + -inc.

strubbelen.e: From Middle Dutch strubbelen, frequentative in -elen of strubben.

slaapmuis.e: Compound of slaap (“sleep”) or slapen (“to sleep”) + muis (“mouse”).

stafrijm.e: Compound of staf + rijm, a nineteenth-century Romantic calque of German Stabreim.

steriliseren.e: Likely borrowed from French stériliser. By surface analysis, steriel + -iseren.

slavink.e: From sla (“lettuce”) + vink (“chaffinch”), so coined by butcher Ton Spoelder in 1962, as he advertised the dish as going well with lettuce. The word vink had been used before in names of meat dishes, such as in blinde vink.

stretchen.e: Borrowed from English stretch. Equivalent to stretch + -en.

scheef aankijken.e: Literally, “to regard askance”. Compare French regarder de travers.

stelpen.e: From Middle Dutch stelpen, closely related to stolpen (“to cover”); compare stolp, stulp.

schuldenrem.e: Compound of schuld (“debt”) + -en- + rem (“brake”), calque of German Schuldenbremse.

slettenhaar.e: The first element is a by-form of sliet (“wooden pole or stake”); the second element is haar (“sandy ridge”). Thus a toponymic surname referring to a place where slieten were produced. Unrelated to slet (“slut”) and haar (“hair”).

spookrijden.e: Compound of spook + rijden; perhaps a back-formation from spookrijder, as that word has analogues in e.g. German Geisterfahrer (see there for more), which may indicate that it came earlier.

slappe lach.e: Literally, “weak laughter”, referring to physical weakness of the body during excessive laughter.

examples: Hou je snater!

schranken.e: From Middle Dutch schranken (“to place crosswise, especially to cross one's legs”), derived from schranke. Compare German schräg (“skewed, oblique, slanted”). Not a direct cognate of German schränken, which is cognate with similarly umlauted Middle Dutch schrenken.

s.def: a male given name from West Frisian

sjouke.e: Borrowed from West Frisian Sjouke, a variant of Sieuwerd, ultimately from Proto-Germanic *Sigiwarduz.

slieren.e: From Middle Dutch slideren, from Old Dutch *slidron, from Proto-West Germanic *slidrōn. The sense "to drag" is influenced by sleuren.

sialia.e: From the genus name, translingual Sialia.

statelijk.e: From staat (“state”) + -e- + lijk (“-ly”). Compare German stattlich.

sermoen.e: From Middle Dutch sermoon, borrowed from Old French sermon, from Latin sermō.

snoozen.e: Borrowed from English snooze with the suffix -en.

sluiken.e: Compared to Proto-Slavic *lyzgati (“to slide, to skate”).

spliceosoom.e: Borrowed from English spliceosome. The stress pattern differs from English and is adapted to other Dutch words ending in -soom.

stroboscoop.e: Borrowed from German Stroboskop. Equivalent to Ancient Greek στρόβος (stróbos) + -scoop.

smelleken.e: A diminutive in -ken of smeerle (“small falcon, merlin”), a form attested until at least as late as the 17th century, which in turn derives from Middle Dutch smeerle, from Old Dutch *smiril, from Proto-West Germanic *smiril, from Proto-Germanic *smirilaz.

serpeling.e: From a by-form of zerp (“sharp, pungent, tangy”) + -e- + -ling.

snipverkouden.e: Compound of snip (“snipe”) + verkouden (“afflicted with a cold”).

spaander.e: Originally the plural of Middle Dutch spâen (whence spaan). Said plural form, spânere, was later reanalysed as a singular form. The addition of /-d-/ between /-n-/ and /-ə(-)/ is a common occurence in Dutch.

soliditeit.e: Borrowed from French solidité. By surface analysis, solide + -iteit.

Hij heeft de lantaarn laag aangestoken. Hij loopt er nu mee in zijn lange jas, met zijn hoge hoed, als een nachtwacht. Of als een schobberdebonk, die langs de deuren met een ster van de Drie Koningen komt zingen.

schobberdebonk.e: Uncertain. Perhaps from schobber + bonk (“large, coarse man”).
